question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any engagement her Department is having with counterparts in the Republic of Ireland’s road authority to understand the potential benefits of increasing the proportion of recycled asphalt planings used in road building and maintenance projects.
To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the activity being delivered through Primary Care Elective Services.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department is considering specifying minimum recycled asphalt planings percentage requirements in Northern Ireland’s road construction and maintenance, in the context of meeting the targets set out in the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether alternative approaches to road construction, including the use of recycled asphalt planings, are being considered for new infrastructure projects.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether the technology exists locally to use recycled asphalt planings, up to 50 per cent in new roads construction projects.
To ask the Minister of Education to outline any assessment that has been made of the need to establish more Specialist Provision in Mainstream Schools units.

To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the estimated value of (i) underpayments; and (ii) overpayments, in pensions discovered during the review of the Civil Service Pension Scheme benefits by the McCloud remedy team.
answer_text: I refer the Member to the answer provided for AQW 43581/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of (i) underpayments; and (ii) overpayments, of pensions discovered during the review of the Northern Ireland Civil Service Pension Scheme benefits by the McCloud remedy team.
answer_text: The McCloud Remedy team’s review of NI Civil Service Pension Scheme benefits is to process the McCloud Remedy Choices.  If, during the McCloud remedy work, the team becomes aware of a potential issue affecting benefits already in payment, it refers the case to the appropriate business area to be reviewed and progressed through normal business-as-usual processes.  Information on overpayments is held centrally by Civil Service Pensions Financial Services Unit. However, it does not record the information at a level that would enable this question to be answered.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any effect that acknowledgment of administrative error has on the requirements for recovery of pension overpayments in line with the guidance for Managing Public Money Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Managing Public Money NI (MPMNI) provides guidance on the recovery of overpayments, including where an overpayment has arisen due to administrative error. Acknowledgement that an overpayment resulted from administrative error does not, of itself, remove the requirement to consider recovery. Where an overpayment is identified, recovery will normally be pursued, applying the Department’s normal business-as-usual processes. Each case is considered on its own merits, in line with MPMNI, to determine whether recovery is appropriate and proportionate, taking account of the Department’s responsibilities for the proper stewardship of public funds and any relevant legal considerations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether new Farm Sustainability Policy and Standards will incorporate farm hedge management, to reflect sustainability principles and prevent non-essential hedge cutting during the bird nesting season.
answer_text: The new Farm Sustainability Standards (FSS) have robust regulations and guidance in place to protect hedgerows and prevent non-essential hedge cutting during the bird nesting season. Any farmer claiming an applicable DAERA farm payment, must abide by FSS.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether any assessment has been made of the enforcement of guidelines for roadside hedge cutting during the closed season.
answer_text: DAERA has an inspection regime in place to protect hedgerows through the new Farm Sustainability Standards (FSS) which were introduced from 1 January 2026. In addition, any farmer or member of the public can report hedge cutting during the closed season to the Department. Where DAERA receives a report of hedge cutting during the closed period, it is investigated and if a breach is identified, the farmer will incur a FSS penalty. The FSS will be subject to periodic review.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail when charities and arm’s-length bodies involved in youth work will be advised of their budget allocation from the Education Authority's local area fund for the remainder of the 2026-2027 financial year, after June.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has confirmed funding for Regional and Local Voluntary Youth Organisations to cover the first quarter’s work until June 2026.  A single composite indicative Block Grant allocation has recently been provided to the EA for both schools and youth services for 2026-2027.  This will bring youth funding into line with other established education budgets and will give the EA the flexibility to make appropriate, evidence-based decisions in respect of its budget in totality. In view of this allocation, I expect the EA will want to provide clarity on the youth funding for the remainder of the 2026-27 financial year in the near future.EA decisions will be in the context of the statutory requirement to secure provision of adequate youth facilities and should align with current and emerging policy direction. A profile of planned youth expenditure will be required to be submitted to the Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail her most current assessment of the York Street Interchange Project as a major road scheme priority.
answer_text: In October 2022, my predecessor published a Placemaking and Active Travel Review report and asked officials to take forward further work to refine the options within it. Officials have completed this work and have engaged with key stakeholders on the York Street Interchange project, to gather their assessment of the report.Officials have presented the findings of this work to me, and I am taking the time to consider the next steps for this key infrastructure project.As the scheme is not funded through City and Growth deals and is not identified as an Executive Flagship project, I have asked my officials to consider possible alternative funding methods which could facilitate the scheme progression. This work is at an early stage and will take several months to complete.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any protocols in place to use and share information contained in (i) Education, Health and Care Plans for children in England; (ii) Individual Development Plans for children in Wales; (iii) Coordinated Support Plans for children in Scotland; (iv) Individual Education Plans for children in the Republic of Ireland, who are being transferred to schools in Northern Ireland and require Statements of Special Educational Needs.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) advise that, when notified of a child or young person transferring from another jurisdiction, it will review the available information to determine whether a placement in a specialist setting is required, and where there is capacity in the required type of setting, will secure a placement pending the outcome of the statutory assessment. The 1998 Code of Practice on the Identification and Assessment of SEN (the Code), Part VI Statements: para 4.7 and 4.9, provides for situations where a child arrives in Northern Ireland with learning difficulties that would normally warrant at a statement.The EA recognise that children and young people with the equivalent of a statement from other jurisdictions would benefit from support whilst the statutory assessment process is carried out, and where appropriate may provide discretionary resourcing available to the school the child or young person attends.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether (i) any assessment has been made of the current regulatory framework governing the wholesale market for home heating oil in Northern Ireland; and (ii) she has any plans to extend the remit of the NI Utility Regulator to include oversight of this sector, in order to protect consumers from excessive price volatility.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline any work carried out to repair and improve sea walls in Rhanbuoy and Belfast Road, Carrickfergus, following damage and flooding caused by recent storms.
answer_text: My officials initially assessed the damage to the sea wall, at Rhanbuoy Park, immediately following Storm Bram, which occurred on Tuesday 9 December 2025.Temporary measures were put in place to ensure public safety and mitigate against future flooding, until permanent repairs can be completed.As you will appreciate work of this nature is susceptible to the elements; therefore, repairs will be scheduled during more amenable weather conditions.Officials continue to carry out ad-hoc inspections at this location, especially following adverse weather events and can advise that no reported incidents were received as a result of Storm Chandra.The repair to the sea revetment along Belfast Road, Carrickfergus is now substantially complete.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of the Education Authority's policy on school crossing patrols.
answer_text: The Education Authority has full responsibility for the recruitment and employment of School Crossing Patrol Officers. All requests for new and replacement SCPs are assessed in line with the processes laid out in the EA’s SCP Scheme guidance, which was approved by my department in 2021. The School Crossing Patrol service is provided by the Education Authority on a discretionary basis.  I have been advised by the EA that its policy and the site assessment process is based on the ‘School Crossing Patrol Service Guidelines’ produced by Road Safety Great Britain. The guidelines are compiled based on existing legislation, best practice, health and safety and case law and are used in other Uk jurisdictions. The criteria within these guidelines enables the EA to assess each site in a consistent manner and prioritise its resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the average time to repair traffic light faults.
answer_text: My department receives a high number of traffic light faults, with around 10,000 reported each year.  Officials record the time a fault was reported and the time the fault was repaired but does not hold individual response times in a readily accessible format.  Any attempt to provide this information would require an extensive exercise to be conducted across the department and this exercise itself would very likely involve disproportionate costs.The Department operates a tiered system of target response times depending on the location of the site. Our target response times range from two hours at peak times at key sites, to eight hours at less critical sites. The contract with our maintenance provider has a requirement that 98% of reports are attended to on time, and I can confirm that this target is consistently met.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the reason why the traffic lights on the A2 Bank Road, Larne, and the Main Road, Glynn, over the single carriageway at Carnduff railway bridge, habitually fail under storm conditions.
answer_text: I have been informed by my officials that there have been a number of faults at the traffic lights on the A2 at Carnduff railway bridge over recent years, the majority of which occurred during the winter months. The latest fault occurred during Storm Chandra on 27 January 2026 and was caused by a Residual Current Device tripping the power supply to the lights. Our maintenance contractor has repaired the lights and will undertake further investigation to determine any areas of vulnerability that can be addressed. Any necessary repairs or equipment replacement will be added to the traffic light maintenance programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any consideration her Department has given to the potential impacts of current parental leave arrangements for multiple birth families on (i) maternal health; (ii) family wellbeing; (iii) gender equality; and (iv) long-term workforce participation.
answer_text: At this time my Department has not undertaken a specific assessment of the potential impacts of current parental leave arrangements for multiple birth families. My Good Jobs Employment Rights Bill will provide enhancements to existing provision for paternity leave and flexible working; and introduce new rights to neo-natal leave and carer’s leave. I consider that collectively, these reforms will help families meet modern life’s demands, support wellbeing, and allow parents and carers to remain in the workforce. In addition, I draw your attention to existing legislation in the north of Ireland which offers unpaid parental leave of up to 18 weeks leave for every qualifying employee for each child up to their 18th birthday. The right applies to each child. Therefore, if an employee has twins, they are entitled to 36 weeks of parental leave. I trust this is of use to current families of multiples.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether her Department has considered reforming parental or shared leave entitlements, to recognise the specific caregiving demands associated with multiple births.
answer_text: At this time, my Department has not considered reforming parental or shared parental leave entitlements to recognise the specific caregiving demands associated with multiple births. My Good Jobs Bill will remove the restriction with regards to paternity leave which will allow a father/partner to take paternity leave or shared parental leave in an order which allows them the greatest flexibility. Parental leave is currently available in the north of Ireland up to the child’s 18th birthday.  Each parent can take 18 weeks unpaid parental leave for each child, which already includes separate entitlements per child in cases of multiple births, as long as the eligibility criteria have been met.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any assessment her Department has made of the adequacy and fairness of current (i) maternity; (ii) parental; and (iii) shared leave, entitlements in relation to parents of twins or other multiple births.
answer_text: My Good Jobs Employment Rights Bill will include a number of family related employment measures relating to paternity leave, neonatal care leave, flexible working and carer’s leave.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) whether her Department has examined comparative parental leave policies, operated by other public sector employers in Northern Ireland, including those that allocate leave on a per-child basis; and (ii) any scope that exists to explore similar approaches within devolved employment policy.
answer_text: Statutory minimum entitlements are intended to establish a base level of entitlement that can be accessed by qualifying employees and provided by all employers.   Within that framework, employers who can do so can offer enhanced provisions that reflect their workforce needs and resources.  My Department is aware that many employers in the North of Ireland, both public and private, operate enhanced parental leave arrangements that go beyond statutory minimum entitlements and I encourage all employers who can go beyond the statutory minimum to do so.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline how they work with HM Government to remove any internal UK trade barriers which have been created and maintained following Brexit and the Windsor Framework.
To ask the Minister of Justice whether Policing and Community Safety Partnerships are allowed to acquire new speed indicator devices for use in their areas from their allocated budget.
answer_text: ANSWERMy Department, working alongside the Northern Ireland Policing Board through the PCSP Joint Committee, provides £4.25 million each year in operational and administrative funding to Policing and Community Safety Partnerships. This funding supports work to address local community safety priorities across council areas, including road safety.  I am aware that PCSPs have previously invested in speed indicator devices, and that there are currently 158 mobile and fixed devices in place across Northern Ireland that have been purchased and are being maintained by PCSPs. While the Department for Infrastructure holds policy responsibility for road safety and does not routinely fund these devices, it has issued guidance, available on its website, on their appropriate use. Given the range of road safety initiatives already in operation, and the need to balance these with other important community safety priorities within a limited budget, the Joint Committee agreed that, while funding may continue to be used to maintain existing devices, no new speed indicator devices should be procured by PCSPs during the 2025/26 financial year using Joint Committee funding.  PCSPs may, however, submit a business case to the Northern Ireland Road Safety Partnership or other potential funders to meet the costs of additional devices. A review of speed indicator devices will be undertaken by the end of the financial year, with input from the PSNI and the Department for Infrastructure. In the meantime, I am aware that officials and partners continue to engage to review current arrangements and ensure they operate as effectively as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any support that can be offered to farmers within the bluetongue Temporary Control Zone to compensate for loss of income and extra costs incurred.
answer_text: Disease control zones are implemented as a disease control response measure and my department is undertaking extensive surveillance and testing, aiming to mitigate against any further disease spread and protect the Bluetongue free area, unaffected farms within the restricted area and the wider Northern Ireland agri-economy. My officials remain in close contact with key stakeholders on next steps for movement controls out of the control zone. My Department has already announced several measures to help farmers, and trade, including the movement of livestock into the zone now being permitted under general licence and the recommencement of livestock moves to Great Britain for livestock from farms outside the temporary control zone.  There is no specific scheme for consequential losses for farms within temporary control zones.	Any further updates regarding movement restrictions will be communicated directly to all stakeholders where appropriate and will be available on the DAERA website at: https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/articles/bluetongue-situation-northern-ireland
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any steps his Department will take to provide timely information to farmers within the bluetongue Temporary Control Zone, in terms of guidance and estimated timescales for lifting restrictions.
answer_text: DAERA veterinary officials are continually assessing the next steps regarding movement restrictions.	You will be aware that from Tuesday 9 December 2025 moves into the Temporary Control Zone (TCZ) are permitted on a General Licence, available on the DAERA website at https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/publications/bluetongue-general-licences A Press Release was issued on this by my Department which can be found at: https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/news/daera-permit-some-animal-movements-under-licence-following-bluetongue-outbreak In addition, moves from the free area within Northern Ireland (NI) to Great Britain (GB) are now also permitted with no requirements beyond those which normally apply.  Those who wish to export livestock to GB should contact their private veterinary practitioner in the normal manner.I can assure you that any further updates regarding the remaining movement restrictions will be communicated in a timely manner and will be available on the DAERA website at: https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/articles/bluetongue-situation-northern-ireland.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail (i) any discussions their Department has had with the UK Government in relation to the end of UK Shared Prosperity Fund (UKSPF); and (ii) any work being done to ensure replacement funding streams to support those currently in receipt of UKSPF funding.
answer_text: We have raised this issue during all our engagements with the British Government and will continue to do so to ensure the ongoing delivery of the important work funded under the current arrangements.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to outline any steps being taken to identify efficiency savings across his and other Executive Departments.
answer_text: I have spoken many times both in the Chamber and around the Executive table of the constrained financial environment within which we are operating.With increasing demands on our public services, it is crucial that we use our limited resources in a sustainable manner. One key strand of our Budget Sustainability work is the development of five-year departmental plans. These plans will set out each department’s strategic direction, over the medium term, outline the costs involved, and identify efficiencies and other opportunities to reduce spending.The goal is clear: to deliver long-term financial sustainability while protecting essential services.I have also submitted practical proposals to the Executive within my policy remit to generate new revenue. A rates paper on both the elevation of the Maximum Capital Value and the reduction of the Early Payment Discount has been brought before the Executive which would raise an additional £9M for public services here. I would encourage other Departments to do likewise. For my part, and recognising the significant financial constraints facing our Budget, the proposals I brought forward last week on enhancing support to Small Businesses was based on generating that revenue on the domestic side of the rating system to fund support on the other. This would mean I don’t have to then seek additional funding for these proposals - however the backing of my Executive colleagues and Members in the Chamber is required to realise that objective.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what assessment has been carried out on the impact of the Education Authority’s decision to increase the cost of school meals by approximately 20 per cent, on low-income families, and those already struggling with the rising cost of living.
answer_text: In taking its decision to raise the price of a school meal for those not entitled to free school meals, the Education Authority (EA) recognised that children and young people having access to a nutritious lunch positively impacted both their educational and health outcomes. The EA acknowledged that if a child is no longer able to access a healthy lunch, it could result in a negative impact on these outcomes for any such children. However, the price has been unchanged since 2017 and will remain significantly below the cost of production. The EA therefore believes a school meal still provides good value for money for a healthy two course lunch.  A family not entitled to free school meals retains the choice of whether to purchase a school meal or to make their own arrangements, such as providing a packed lunch. To support parents in this, the Public Health Agency (PHA) publication “Are you packing a healthy lunch?” provides advice and guidance to parents to ensure their child is able to access an affordable, healthy and nutritionally balanced packed lunch. Consequently, if a family decides not to pay for a school meal, the availability of this guidance on preparing a healthy packed lunch means that all parents should be able to ensure that children are able to access an affordable, healthy lunch, either through the canteen or a packed lunch. The PHA publication is available at: https://www.publichealth.hscni.net/publications/are-you-packing-healthy-lunch-english-and-irish-translation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline any steps her Department is taking to maximise apprenticeship opportunities for students currently in further education.
answer_text: Apprenticeships are employment opportunities determined by employer needs and developing our apprenticeship system is a key priority. Collaborative efforts, including sectoral, regional, and cross-border partnerships, are creating clearer pathways and supporting the growth of the apprenticeship system with 200 pathways available and investment by the Department of over £48m annually.Participation is at a record level with over fourteen thousand apprentices presently enrolled in programmes offered by my Department, reflecting an increase in both employer and participant engagement. While apprenticeships have grown in popularity and success, challenges remain for young people seeking employment in specific sectors.FE Colleges are integral to preparing young people for vocational pathways, providing the skills, knowledge, and experience required for progression, especially in priority skills areas. Colleges work with councils, sector bodies, and regional initiatives to align support with local economic needs and learner priorities.Annual initiatives such as Apprenticeship Week and the Apprenticeship Awards promote the value and visibility of apprenticeships, encouraging wider participation. The Apprenticeship Action Plan aims to increase employer engagement, with measures like piloting Apprenticeship Navigators to assist SMEs recruiting their first apprentice.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any discussions he has had with the Education Authority regarding alternative cost-saving measures, that could have been taken to avoid increasing the price of school meals.
answer_text: My Department continues to engage closely with the Education Authority (EA)  in seeking to ensure that the EA Catering Service operates as efficiently as possible. A key aspect of this work is to maximise footfall of pupils paying for a school meal. The additional income from more pupils choosing to take a school meal reduces the overall net cost to the EA of operating its school meal service. The EA Catering Service has been successful in recent years in increasing pupil footfall through a range of initiatives informed by a detailed engagement exercise undertaken with pupils, parents, schools and catering staff. This has seen revised menus, promotions, branding, theme days, improved communication with parents and efforts to reduce queuing time – all aimed at creating a more appealing school meals offering to pupils. As a result, there has been a sustained increase in pupils choosing a school meal. An example of this has been the “Eat Smart” theme week operated by the EA Catering Service in September 2025 across more than 500 primary schools. While these initiatives have been successful in increasing footfall and therefore the EA’s income from school meals, the scale of the anticipated shortfall in the EA’s overall budget this year has required it to make a number of difficult and unpalatable decisions aimed at reducing this shortfall. Having maintained its school meals prices at the same level since 2017/18, despite the significant rises in its production costs over that period, the EA determined that a price increase for school meals was unavoidable in the current financial circumstances.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she plans to undertake a review of the Explosives (Fireworks) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2002.
answer_text: I have no current plans to review the existing fireworks licensing arrangements.  The existing regulations provide a robust framework that seeks to ensure fireworks are sold and bought responsibly for use by the public. My Department faces significant resourcing pressures which requires us to prioritise those areas of highest risk/greatest need.In recognition of the public concern around the misuse of fireworks, I have instructed my officials to examine penalties and enforcement concerning the illegal use or sale or fireworks in the pending review of sentencing issues.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he plans to review the Education Authority’s decision to raise the cost of school meals.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) recently announced a number of savings measures that seek to reduce an anticipated significant budget shortfall. The scale of this shortfall has required the EA to make a number of difficult and unpalatable decisions, including its decision to raise its school meals prices.The EA continues to support school-age children through the provision of free school meals to low-income families. This decision taken by the EA will not impact the nearly 90,000 children and young people who are able to access a free school meal each day. While it is regrettable that an increase in the EA’s school meal prices has been necessary, it is important to note that the EA has maintained the same school meal prices since 2017/18 despite significant rises in costs during that period. This approach was no longer sustainable in the current budgetary circumstances. As the EA is responsible for managing its budget and given that its decision is seeking to reduce its anticipated budget deficit, it would not be appropriate for me to intervene in the EA’s determination of its school meal prices.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AWQ 25480/22-27 and AQW 25481/22-27, to detail the outcomes, following preliminary scoping work on the Review of the Implementation of the Planning Act, in relation to advertising and consulting the public on planning applications.
answer_text: I can reiterate my commitment to the action in the first report on the Review of the Implementation of the Planning Act (RIPA) to undertake a review of the publicity and advertisement requirements in planning.Preliminary scoping work has included examination of the approaches to planning advertisement in other jurisdictions in addition to engagement with local councils and the Planning Appeals Commission to establish the nature and extent of the costs incurred by them in publicising notification of planning applications. This work is continuing.It is critically important to gather the best available contemporary evidence and data, including the outcomes from on-going stakeholder engagements, to ensure informed decisions around policy development and options in regard to this aspect of the planning process. This will ultimately culminate in public consultation on any policy proposals.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any assessment her Department has made of the impact of the irresponsible use of fireworks on communities, including effects on (i) public safety; (ii) vulnerable residents; (iii) pets; and (iv) people living with trauma or anxiety.
answer_text: Whilst I have not commissioned a specific assessment of the impact of irresponsible use of fireworks, I am very mindful of their impact when the permitted framework for their use is not followed. The existing fireworks licensing arrangements provide a robust framework that seeks to ensure fireworks are sold and bought responsibly for use by the public.  There is a balance to be struck between those who enjoy fireworks and those who do not, and I encourage people to be mindful and considerate of vulnerable people and animals.The licensing system in particular aids partners, including PSNI, to identify unlicensed and illegal fireworks use.  Offences committed under the firework regulations may result in a fine of up to £5,000, or a prison sentence, or both.Any concerns about the misuse of fireworks should be reported to the PSNI.Policing and Community Safety Partnerships (PCSPs), funded by my Department and the NI Policing Board, play a key role in the Department’s operational response to community safety concerns at a local level.Tackling antisocial behaviour is a key strategic priority for PCSPs, which engage with local communities, statutory agencies and the community and voluntary sector to develop planned, purposeful, structured interventions to address the issue in each Council area.Although we are not aware of any specific fireworks interventions by PCSPs, they support partner messaging on social media, such as the PSNI Halloween safety campaign. Some PCSP initiatives include on street youth intervention and community safety wardens who assist in addressing the issue of ASB in local areas.In recognition of the public concern around the misuse of fireworks, I have instructed my officials to examine penalties and enforcement concerning the illegal use or sale or fireworks in the pending review of sentencing issues.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any investment being made to enhance (i) digital learning; (ii) technology access; and (iii) science, technology, engineering and mathematics, resources in schools, in East Antrim.
answer_text: My Department does not hold the information in the specific terms requested, nor does it break down expenditure by constituency.  I can advise that my Department’s total expenditure on ICT and related initiatives over the past seven financial years is in the order of £184m.  In addition, during 2024/25, my Department provided £240k of Extended Schools funding to 16 schools in the East Antrim area. Two of these schools included ICT and Key Skills provision as part of their Extended Schools programmes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any plans her Department has to introduce or strengthen penalties for the misuse of fireworks, including measures to deter anti-social behaviour associated with fireworks sales or use.
answer_text: I have no current plans to review the existing fireworks licensing arrangements.  The existing regulations provides a robust framework that seeks to ensure fireworks are sold and bought responsibly for use by the public.The licensing system aids partners, including PSNI, to identify unlicensed and illegal fireworks use.  Offences committed under the firework regulations may result in a fine of up to £5,000, or a prison sentence, or both.Any concerns about the misuse of fireworks should be reported to the PSNI.In recognition of the public concern around the misuse of fireworks, I have instructed my officials to examine penalties and enforcement concerning the illegal use or sale or fireworks in the pending review of sentencing issues.Policing and Community Safety Partnerships (PCSPs), funded by my Department and the NI Policing Board, play a key role in the Department’s operational response to community safety concerns at a local level.Tackling antisocial is a key strategic priority for PCSPs, which engage with local communities, statutory agencies and the community and voluntary sector to develop planned, purposeful, structured interventions to address the issue in each Council area.Although we are not aware of any specific fireworks interventions by PCSPs, they support partner messaging on social media, such as the PSNI Halloween safety campaign.Some PCSP initiatives include on street youth intervention and community safety wardens who assist in addressing the issue of ASB in local areas.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any support being provided to schools to help (i) recruit; and (ii) retain, teaching and classroom support staff, in East Antrim.
answer_text: The Department of Education is not the employer of teachers or any other school-based staff. Teachers and support staff are employed by the Board of Governors (BoG) of the school at which they work and, where applicable, the employing authority i.e. the Education Authority (EA) for controlled schools and the Council for Catholic Maintained Schools (CCMS) for Catholic maintained schools.  EA and CCMS provide advice and guidance to BoG on recruitment. The support being provide by both EA and CCMS is the same for their respective schools across the whole of Northern Ireland and there is nothing specific provided to schools in East Antrim which differs from other schools or geographical areas.The EA and CCMS were asked to provide input for the response to this question, and a summary of their input has been included below:Council for Catholic Maintained Schools (CCMS)(i) Recruitment: CCMS continues to provide direct support to Catholic maintained schools in the recruitment process, including the development and advertisement of teaching through their centralised recruitment platform. Schools are also supported with guidance on job descriptions, shortlisting, and interview processes to ensure consistency, transparency and compliance with employment legislation. (ii) Retention: Through engagement with the Department of Education, EA and the teaching unions, CCMS continues to advocate for measures that support staff retention, including workload management, opportunities for professional growth, and flexible employment arrangements where appropriate. CCMS is committed to ensuring that schools in East Antrim and across the Catholic maintained sector can continue to attract, develop, and retain high-quality teaching staff. Education Authority (EA)EA is committed to ensuring that all teaching and classroom support staff are recruited and retained in roles that provide job security, fair and consistent pay, accredited training and defined career progression.A significant step to delivering this came with the recent implementation of the EA Pay & Grading Review for Non-Teaching staff.  This significant investment in staff delivered pay increases for classroom support staff of between 5.3% and 14.4% and ensured all staff reach the top of their pay scale within 3 years.In relation to the teaching staff, agreement was reached on the 2024/25 Pay Award in April 2025, with a 5.5% pay uplift paid in May 25 and a workload review agreed through an independent panel.  The following support is provided to all schools across Northern Ireland, including East Antrim, where EA is the employing authority.(i) Recruitment: EA’s Resourcing Service provides recruitment support and guidance to schools for teaching and classroom support staff.    Examples of this include:Successful delivery of an improvement programme for the end-to-end school recruitment process to address school leader feedback and ensure an efficient, flexible and regionally consistent service.Review of th

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy how her Department plans to encourage entrepreneurship among young people and recent graduates, particularly in partnership with local colleges and universities.
answer_text: Promoting and developing entrepreneurship in younger people is vitally important and aligns with my key priorities, particularly the promotion of regional balance. Our Further Education (FE) Colleges’ Entrepreneurship Hub is currently implementing several strategies in all FE colleges to embed entrepreneurship into the curriculum.In addition, the Entrepreneurship Hub is also driving entrepreneurial thinking by engaging with external stakeholders. The Future Founders programme is a prime example currently being delivered in all six colleges.I am committed to ensuring that higher education provision is aligned with the needs of the local economy. To achieve this, Outcome Agreements have been established between my Department and the universities, outlining how each institution contributes to Departmental goals such as raising productivity and closing the skills gap. Through these agreements, the universities have committed to enhancing their links with business and industry to ensure graduates are being produced with the skills needed for key sectors of the economy, including developing the attitudes and behaviours that foster an entrepreneurial approach.Additionally, I have recently established an SME Entrepreneurship Codesign Group that is tasked with developing an action plan containing recommendations to support the creation and growth of small businesses across the north.  Membership of this group includes representatives from Young Enterprise NI, Northern Regional College, and the University of Ulster.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any discussions her Department has had with (i) the Department for Communities; and (ii) local councils on supporting town centre regeneration projects that promote local business growth and job creation, since the beginning of the 2022-2027 Assembly mandate.
answer_text: Town centre regeneration is the responsibility of the Department for Communities. Officials have ongoing discussions with their counterparts in Department for Communities on issues of common interest including town centre regeneration. My officials have regular contact with all councils as part of Local Economic Partnerships to discuss local business growth and job creation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) any capital investment that has been made in schools; and (ii) any further planned projects, in the East Antrim constituency, in each of the last three years.
answer_text: (i) Details of capital expenditure for schools in the East Antrim constituency for each of the last three financial years is set out in tables which I have deposited in the Assembly Library.(ii) Details of any further planned projects are also set out in tables which I have deposited in the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the work her Department is undertaking to improve access to finance and investment opportunities for start-ups and early-stage businesses across Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Invest NI’s Access to Finance programme provides a suite of debt and equity funds for start-ups, micro businesses and SMEs, across the North whose growth is constrained by a lack of traditional finance. The funds, which operate on a fully commercial basis, are provided to improve access to finance, and unlock investment opportunities, especially for scaling, start-up and early-stage businesses.Invest NI has committed over £100 million to the current suite of Access to Finance funds and, when combined with targeted private leverage of £150 million, over £250 million will be invested into start-ups and SMEs across the North over the next ten years.  The Access to Finance programme commenced in 2011 and has invested over £200 million in over 1,250 businesses to date.  This investment has helped unlock private sector funding of over £400 million, resulting in over £600 million of investment into our economy.To raise awareness of these funds, Invest NI has run ten regional awareness sessions over the past year from its regional offices network. It also continues to work with organisations such as British Business Bank and Intertrade Ireland to raise awareness of the breadth of finance available for SMEs and has tasked the individual Fund Managers to undertake marketing activities across the North to promote each of their funds.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether her Department has any plans to expand local enterprise hubs or business incubator programmes to foster collaboration, innovation and entrepreneurship in towns and rural areas.
answer_text: Invest NI works with operators of innovation hubs across the North to promote services, drive new business, and host events. It also supports entrepreneurship and business growth through its regional offices in Newry, Omagh, Derry, Belfast, and Ballymena, focusing on innovation, upskilling, and exporting.		To strengthen local economies, I have established eleven Local Economic Partnerships (LEPs) backed by a £45m Regional Growth Fund. These partnerships, comprising councils, businesses, and community representatives, are developing action plans to drive local economic growth. Each LEP sets its own priorities, which may include capital support for enterprise hubs or tailored business programmes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the current estimated investment required for identifying and building solutions to capacity issues in wastewater treatment in the Larne catchment area, to allow new connections to be added to the system.
answer_text: The issue you have raised is an operational matter for NI Water; however, I have sought the required information.NI Water has advised my department that the current high-level estimate for the upgrade of Storm Overflow assets within the Larne catchment would be in the order of £120m - £140m. NI Water has further advised that this figure may change should the Environmental Regulator agree to derogations the company has requested for both the Wastewater Treatment Works and the Storm Overflow assets within the Larne catchment, as this may dictate the design of any capital upgrades required.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com.Making direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, in light of the decade-long identification of hydraulic incapacity issues associated with the Larne Wastewater Treatment Works at Redlands, to detail the progress that has been made to (i) identify, commission and build new treatment facilities; (ii) increase network storage; (iii) find pumping solutions; and (iv) maximise storm water separation and sustainable drainage systems, within the Larne catchment area.
answer_text: The management of public wastewater infrastructure is an operational matter for NI Water. However, I have sought the requested information from NI Water and have been advised as follows:NI Water acknowledges the long-standing hydraulic capacity challenges associated with the Larne Wastewater Treatment Works (WwTW) at Redlands. While the works currently complies with the sanitary requirements of its Water Order Consent, issued by the Northern Ireland Environment Agency (NIEA), hydraulic limitations persist due to the unique treatment process, originally developed following a Public Inquiry which constrained the footprint of the site.  (i)  	During the early stages of Price Control 21, NI Water initiated investigations into potential upgrade options for Larne WwTW. However, progress was limited due to the need to complete Integrated Environmental Modelling (IEM) and Drainage Area Plan (DAP) modelling for the catchment. These models are essential to inform a holistic, sustainable solution for the Larne and Blackcave catchments. To date, NI Water has completed a capacity assessment of the works and population flow/load determination, developed short-term headroom increase concepts using new technologies, undertaken site selection and performance reviews and scoped base maintenance and short-term solutions. As a result, NI Water is now considering proposals for a sustainable, catchment-wide solution and once a suitable solution is identified, construction would be subject to the availability of funding, planning, land acquisition, stakeholder engagement, and regulatory approvals. (ii) 	NI Water has built a hydraulic model, developed and calibrated for the Larne sewer catchment. The scale of solutions generated by this process represented a significant capital investment requirement (ca: £120 - 140m), therefore, NI Water has sought to achieve more sustainable catchment outcomes through a parallel Integrated Environmental Modelling (IEM) study. The baseline results from the IEM modelling have been presented to NIEA and will define the design parameters for any capital upgrades required. (iii)	NI Water has undertaken a full survey of wastewater pumping stations and the catchment’s pumping regime. Investigations into saline ingress and flow pass forward rates are ongoing. These assessments will inform future pumping upgrades, aimed at improving hydraulic performance and supporting wastewater capacity enhancements. (iv)	Investigations are ongoing in relation to areas for stormwater separation within the catchment however, these are likely to be limited due to the level of existing separate sewerage systems. I would add that my Department’s Water, Sustainable Drainage and Flood Management Bill, which is currently going through the Assembly, will allow my Department to make regulations in relation to the design, approval, operation and maintenance of sustainable drainage systems (SuDS).  In tandem with this, my Department is carrying out a public consultatio

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether the Transport Regulation Unit carries out the same role, in terms of (i) regulating goods vehicle operators; (ii) issuing licenses; and (iii) enforcing safety and reliability standards, similar to the Traffic Commissioners for Great Britain.
answer_text: The Transport Regulation Unit carries out a similar role to the Traffic Commissioners for Great Britain, including regulating goods vehicle operators, issuing licenses. Enforcing safety and reliability standards is a function performed by Driver and Vehicle Standards Agency in Great Britain and the Driver Vehicle Agency in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the rationale as to why Heavy Goods Vehicle and Public Service Vehicle Operator public inquiry decisions, rather than outcomes, are not routinely published online by the Transport Regulation Unit.
answer_text: The Transport Regulation Unit do not regulate buses or coaches. The Department does not have the legal vires to publish public inquiry decisions in relation to Heavy Goods Vehicles.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she will review the detail of public inquiry decisions, published by the Transport Regulation Unit, in light of the position in the rest of the United Kingdom, under the Traffic Commissioners for Great Britain.
answer_text: Unlike the Traffic Commissioner for Britain, the Department does not have the legal vires to publish public inquiry decisions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) how frequently gullies and drains are cleared on the Shore Road, Larne; and (ii) whether they are cleared more frequent in autumn, to prevent build up of leaves.
answer_text: My Department places high importance on the need to adequately maintain the road network through day-to-day essential maintenance activities such as gully emptying, to maintain the structural integrity of the road network. However, allocations of funding to maintain our roads have been less than required for many years as a result of British Government austerity. Therefore, my Department has only been able to deliver a limited service across a range of functions including gully emptying.My Department’s current Limited-Service Policy aims to inspect and clean, where necessary, all gullies once annually.Further ad-hoc cleaning may also be undertaken to address any specific localised issues as required, within available budgets, and in advance of and in response to severe weather events. In recognition of the importance of this area of work, I have allocated further funding to deliver additional works in the form of cleaning and maintenance of drainage lines and manholes.The value of our infrastructure cannot be overstated, and I will continue to work with Executive colleagues to ensure appropriate investment is made so my Department can deliver important aspects of road maintenance, such as gully cleaning, ensuring public safety and an efficient transport network.I have been advised that the next scheduled clean of the gullies on Shore Road, Larne will be undertaken in the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, in light of the frequent flooding of the Shore Road, Larne, to detail the work her Department plans to undertake to help prevent future flooding.
answer_text: My officials have determined that the flooding along the Shore Road, Larne is caused by material obstructing a drainage culvert.  The removal of the blockage will require a road closure, as a section of the culvert will need to be removed to clear the obstruction.  Given the nature of this work, favourable weather conditions will be required to complete the removal.The Shore Road is subject to a monthly inspection regime and my officials have also included an instruction to check the culvert for obvious signs of obstruction during these inspections, to provide a more proactive response to the issue.Officials are also reviewing the hydrological capacity of the culvert to determine if an increased pipe size is potentially required and feasible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of the Education Authority's handling of school closures, and the associated communication, during Storm Amy.
answer_text: The decision to close schools, or in this case, to close schools early, is never an easy one and is only ever taken in the interests of safety. The risks that Storm Amy presented meant that early closures were unavoidable.Whilst the decision was made in the interest of public safety, concerns have been raised regarding the decision-making process, the timing and communication of the announcement, and the proportionality of the closures. I therefore instructed my Permanent Secretary to undertake a review of the decision-making process and the communications strategy that was employed. The review was completed at the end of last week and I will consider the report and the recommendations contained within it.It is essential that we learn from each event to ensure that emergency protocols are appropriate and adequate to deal with future storms or severe weather events.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the Criminal Damage Compensation Scheme.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether the Driver and Vehicle Agency is subject to the UK General Data Protection Regulation.
answer_text: As an executive agency of the Department for Infrastructure, the Driver and Vehicle Agency is subject to the UK General Data Protection Regulation. This regulation sets out clear principles for the lawful and responsible handling of personal data. It requires that data be processed fairly and transparently, collected only for specific and legitimate purposes, kept accurate and up to date, stored only as long as necessary, and protected through appropriate security measures. The DVA must also be able to demonstrate compliance with these principles, ensuring accountability in all aspects of its data processing activities.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for their assessment of the threshold for matters to be cross-cutting, significant or controversial, therefore subject to Executive approval.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of her Department's 2025-26 budget allocation to structural roads maintenance, given the current condition of the road network and the recommendations in the Review of the Structural Maintenance Funding Requirements for the Department for Infrastructure (Roads) report.
answer_text: My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government.There has been historic underfunding for the maintenance of the road network for many years, and I am keen to improve our infrastructure with the limited funding available to me as it delivers real benefits for our citizens, our communities and the economy. The ‘Review of the Structural Maintenance Funding Requirements for the Department for Infrastructure (Roads)’ made five recommendations, the main one relating to the level and timing of funding made available for structural maintenance of the road network. Greater certainty of budgets, that meet the level of funding recommended in the report, would allow my Department to bring forward much more effective and properly resourced maintenance programmes. The report suggests it should be entirely possible to achieve significant efficiencies if adequate long-term budgets were in place. These savings might allow the structural maintenance budget to be reduced longer term or, better still, allow my Department to make a much needed impact on reducing the maintenance backlog, which would, in turn, yield even greater efficiencies.My Department submits bids in line with the recommendation and has included a pragmatic ramping up approach which reflects the capacity of internal and external resources. There are many competing financial demands across my Department and the Executive, and it will be challenging to address years of underfunding; however, I am committed to improving our roads here and I continue to pursue additional funding for investment in the road network.I will continue to make the case for investment in infrastructure here, which includes improving the quality of our road network.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the steps she is taking to secure additional funding for structural roads maintenance, given (i) the overall increase in the Department's capital budget; (ii) the low starting level of £68 million, allocated to structural maintenance for the 2025-26 financial year; and (iii) the lack of any further allocations to date.
answer_text: My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government. I am committed to improving our infrastructure as it delivers real benefits for our citizens, our communities and the economy.At the outset of the financial year, I overplanned against my Department's 	opening 2025-26 capital budget, allowing funding to be maximised at the outset. Had I not taken such action, the impact would likely have been greater. As part of my final opening 2025-26 budget decisions, I fully considered the many competing priorities across my Department and ALBs, which all need to be managed within a finite budget.In June Monitoring, my Department bid for additional funding for Structural Maintenance. While this bid was not successful, I continue to make the case for additional funding. In addition, I am keen to look at ways of doing more with the allocations available to me and to look at doing things differently, using technology and trialling new techniques.I have also set out my Department’s investment needs as part of future budget exercises and will continue to engage with my Executive colleagues on the investment required in our infrastructure.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether the Protection of Freedoms Act 2012 applies in Northern Ireland, in relation to requests from third parties to access the details of registered keepers of motor vehicles from the Driver and Vehicle Agency, in relation to private car park charges.
answer_text: The Driver and Vehicle Licensing Agency (DVLA) in Swansea is responsible for vehicle licensing and registration here.   Requests from third parties for details of registered keepers in the North is therefore a matter for DVLA.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether the Driver and Vehicle Agency is obliged to provide the contact details of registered owners of vehicles to third parties, pursuing parking charges on private land.
answer_text: The Driver and Vehicle Licensing Agency (DVLA) in Swansea is responsible for vehicle licensing and registration here.   Requests from third parties for details of registered keepers in the North is therefore a matter for DVLA.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of private car parking firms contracted to manage public (i) on-street; and (ii) off-street parking.
answer_text: Under legislation, my Department is responsible for the management and enforcement of on-street parking. To assist in the delivery of these functions, my Department has one contracted enforcement service provider that provides on-street parking enforcement in towns and cities across the North and at three Park & Ride sites in Belfast. Responsibility for the operation, enforcement and management of car parks owned by local councils is a matter for that council. The Department has no role. There are a number of privately owned car parks, operating on private land, across  the north, which are open to the public. The management and enforcement of these sites is a matter for the owner. The Department has no role. There is currently no legislation in place which specifically applies to the licensing of private car parking firms.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail who holds responsibility to licence private car parking firms, contracted to manage public on-street and off-street parking.
answer_text: Under legislation, my Department is responsible for the management and enforcement of on-street parking. To assist in the delivery of these functions, my Department has one contracted enforcement service provider that provides on-street parking enforcement in towns and cities across the North and at three Park & Ride sites in Belfast. Responsibility for the operation, enforcement and management of car parks owned by local councils is a matter for that council. The Department has no role. There are a number of privately owned car parks, operating on private land, across  the north, which are open to the public. The management and enforcement of these sites is a matter for the owner. The Department has no role. There is currently no legislation in place which specifically applies to the licensing of private car parking firms.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline the changes made to driving (i) lessons; and (ii) tests, for provisional driving licences since 2017, as a result of the Road Traffic (Amendment) Act (Northern Ireland) 2016 and subsequent Graduated Driver Licensing monitoring reports.
answer_text: The Road Traffic (Amendment) Act (NI) 2016 provides the statutory framework for Graduated Driver Licensing (GDL) and will require a suite of subordinate legislation to give it effect.  Once introduced, this legislation will provide a new foundation for improving driving standards including a more structured programme of training and a logbook in which progress must be evidenced.  The training will remain largely practical on-road training with learners practicing in a range of situations and road conditions over a minimum six-month period. Changes to the practical driving test will be required to ensure that it reflects the training programme and effectively assesses the full range of knowledge, skills and behaviours required to drive safely. Preparatory work is ongoing on the significant operational planning and subordinate legislation changes that are required prior to the introduction of GDL. That work is advancing well and I expect to be able to announce the anticipated date that GDL will be introduced shortly.The GDL Monitoring Report does not make recommendations.  It provides a statistical baseline so that when GDL is introduced its impact and effectiveness can be monitored and assessed.  The most recent Monitoring Report for 2023 was published in March 2025. The 2024 Monitoring Report is scheduled to be published in spring 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the sections of the Road Traffic (Amendment) Act (Northern Ireland) 2016, that have yet to be implemented in relation to Graduated Driver Licensing.
answer_text: The statutory framework for the introduction of Graduated Driver Licensing (GDL) is set out in Part 3, sections 16 to 19 of the Road Traffic (Amendment) Act (Northern Ireland) 2016. These sections will require a suite of subordinate legislation to give them effect – specifically a commencement Order and three sets of Regulations, one of which will require Assembly debate.  Once the subordinate legislation comes into effect it will introduce:•   a Mandatory Minimum Learning Period of 6-months;removal of the 45mph speed restriction on learner and newly qualified drivers (enabling learning on the motorway);a learner Logbook and approved Programme of Training;passenger restrictions for newly qualified drivers (applicable to new drivers aged under 24, between the hours of 11pm and 6am, for a 6-month period); a new ‘R’ plate (post-test); andan increase in the restricted period, from one to two years.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to outline any work being carried out to ensure maximum savings to the public purse from combined purchasing powers and economies of scale.
answer_text: Construction and Procurement Delivery have a long established collaborative procurement strategy that combines purchasing powers to achieve economies of scale and maximise savings to the public purse.They have established more than 66 collaborative arrangements for the public sector for common supplies, services and works. These include for example energy, fuel, facilities management, waste, IT hardware and financial services.These collaborative arrangements result in better value for money and reduce procurement transaction costs for other public sector organisations.But it is important to point out that public procurement is not about a race to the bottom line, it is about value for money which incorporates economic, social and environmental benefits.Our collaborative arrangements also incorporate social and environmental benefits and contracts are set up following a human rights-based approach.This ensures that appropriate minimum standards are set, that contracts are fair and sustainable; and that our contractors are ethical and respect human rights.Work is ongoing to encourage more participation and opportunities in all public sector collaborative arrangements and contracts for SMEs, micro businesses and social enterprises; and to maximise social value impact.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when the 24/7 regional thrombectomy centre at the Royal Victoria Hospital will be fully operational.
answer_text: It is estimated that just over £5 million recurrent funding is required to expand the current thrombectomy service to a 24/7 service. While I remain committed to the establishment of a 24/7 service, the financial challenges imposed on my Department’s budget mean that I am unable to allocate this funding. Consequently, I am currently unable to provide a timescale for the implementation of 24/7 thrombectomy service. In the interim I am committed to ensuring that as many people as possible benefit from the service within the current hours of operation. To this end, it should be noted that the thrombectomy rate in Northern Ireland in 2023/24 was 5.9%, higher than the regional rates in England, Scotland and Wales.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether the Public Health Agency will consider relaunching an advertising campaign for Act FAST stroke awareness campaign on social media channels.
answer_text: I recognise the success of the Public Health Agency’s Act FAST campaign in helping people to recognise the signs of having a stroke and promoting the need to call 999 immediately if someone is showing signs of a stroke. Unfortunately, budgetary pressures imposed upon my Department require a pause on most health-related advertising spend.  Consequently, I am unable to commit the funding required to rerun the Act FAST campaign at this time. The Public Health Agency, however, continues to promote Act FAST awareness messaging through non-paid for channels including social media platforms. This has included promotion of Act FAST messaging throughout May 2025 to coincide with Stroke Awareness Month. In addition to this, NI Direct also ran social media posts throughout May to raise awareness of Act FAST messaging.My Department’s press office will continue to work with the communications teams in the PHA and Health and Social Care Trusts to develop a series of social media posts across all platforms to promote Act FAST throughout the year. This includes plans for further social media messaging to coincide with World Stroke Day on 29 October 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline any steps being taken to improve the profitability of farm produce in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The Sustainable Agriculture Programme (SAP), which I launched in January 2025, will provide options for all farm businesses in Northern Ireland to secure and improve their viability and environmental sustainability.  A number of Schemes within the SAP have already commenced and I have also agreed the timeline for the roll out of further schemes over the next two years (subject to necessary business case agreements and legislation in place). Under the Farming for Sustainability - Knowledge Transfer Scheme – All Farm Businesses will have access to information and training linked to supporting enhanced productivity. Business Sustainability Groups and Themed Groups will offer the opportunity for farmers to use knowledge, co-operation and innovation to improve the sustainability and performance of their businesses.Other schemes under the SAP will serve as data platforms to provide important information to help farm businesses e.g. Soil Nutrient Health Scheme, the Bovine Genetics Project and the Carbon Footprinting Project. In addition, others will provide payments through schemes for: Farm Sustainability Payment, Sustainable Farming Investment Scheme, Protein Crop Scheme, Beef Sustainability Package, Farming with Nature Package, Farming for the Generations Programme, Horticulture Schemes and Supply Chain Scheme.I have also committed almost £240,000 this year through my department’s Northern Ireland Regional Food Programme to a wide range of projects that will directly promote locally produced, high quality food. In 2025, this Programme is supporting twenty different events, including the Food Pavilions at five of our local Agricultural Shows, to raise the profile and consumption of our region’s food and drink.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline any representations he has made to the Minister of Finance in relation to borrowing powers for the Housing Executive in order to build new social housing.
answer_text: I have made the case to urgently progress NIHE borrowing - to enable increased investment in homes and to add to new homes – on numerous occasions. I discussed this issue with the Finance Minister, John O’Dowd MLA, on 29th April 2025, and I also met with the former Finance Minister, Dr. Caoimhe Archibald MLA, to discuss this previously. I welcome the recent announcement in the Spending Review and the confirmation that the UK Government and Northern Ireland Executive have agreed to immediately begin negotiations on a full Northern Ireland Executive Fiscal Framework, with the scope of negotiations to include Northern Ireland Housing Executive borrowing.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for their assessment of the work of the Northern Ireland Bureau in Washington.

To ask the Minister of Education to detail what further steps his Department is taking to support schools and teachers with the delivery of CPR training, now that it is part of the post-primary curriculum.
answer_text: As you are aware, legislation introduced in March 2022 requires Cardiopulmonary Resuscitation (CPR) and the use of an Automated External Defibrillator (AED) be included as part of the Learning for Life and Work curriculum at Key Stage 3. In response, my Department has developed the Community of Lifesavers Programme, in partnership with the Council for the Curriculum, Examinations and Assessment (CCEA), the Education Authority (EA), and the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service (NIAS).This programme is overseen by a stakeholder group and is currently working with the Education and Training Inspectorate to explore how the delivery of training can be effectively evaluated. Every post-primary school has received a Community of Lifesavers resource pack, which includes CPR manikins and a training defibrillator unit. In November 2024, the Community of Lifesavers interactive phone app launched, designed to build pupils’ confidence in developing vital CPR and AED skills.In addition, the Save a Life for Northern Ireland (SALNI) partnership has recently been established to bring together organisations committed to improving outcomes from out-of-hospital cardiac arrests. The partnership aims to increase survival rates through coordinated efforts in CPR education, public awareness, and community readiness.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline what his Department is doing to support cricket clubs and cricket unions in Northern Ireland.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department will make an assessment regarding a 20mph speed limit, with appropriate signage, at Islandmagee Primary School, East Antrim.
answer_text: A review of the initiative introducing part-time 20mph speed limits at schools has been undertaken and is available on my Department’s internet site https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/review-part-time-20mph-speed-limits-schools. The review recommends the provision of a third tranche of part-time 20 mph speed limits at schools, however this will be more modest in size and is dependent on the availability of funding. The report also concludes that a more targeted approach is needed to ensure that the correct safety intervention is selected and work to update our policy document is underway.   I have asked officials to prepare the programme of schools to be treated in the next financial year based on the existing scored listing, but with preference given to rural schools which are located on roads where the national speed limit applies. I would also like to see what can be done to improve attitudes to speeding, not only for road safety purposes, but to make places cleaner and greener.  I have therefore asked my officials to prepare a paper on the use of permanent 20mph speed limits particularly near schools, in predominantly residential areas and places such as town centres where there is a higher number of those walking, wheeling and cycling.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department will make an assessment regarding the implementation of a 20mph speed limit, with appropriate signage, at Oakfield Primary School, Carrickfergus.
answer_text: A review of the initiative introducing part-time 20mph speed limits at schools has been undertaken and is available on my Department’s internet site https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/review-part-time-20mph-speed-limits-schools. The review recommends the provision of a third tranche of part-time 20 mph speed limits at schools, however this will be more modest in size and is dependent on the availability of funding. The report also concludes that a more targeted approach is needed to ensure that the correct safety intervention is selected and work to update our policy document is underway.   I have asked officials to prepare the programme of schools to be treated in the next financial year based on the existing scored listing, but with preference given to rural schools which are located on roads where the national speed limit applies. I would also like to see what can be done to improve attitudes to speeding, not only for road safety purposes, but to make places cleaner and greener.  I have therefore asked my officials to prepare a paper on the use of permanent 20mph speed limits particularly near schools, in predominantly residential areas and places such as town centres where there is a higher number of those walking, wheeling and cycling.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on progress made to reduce the expenditure on agency staff across the health and social care service.
answer_text: In October 2022, the then Health Minister, Robin Swann stated that “off contract” expenditure was not a cost-effective use of taxpayers’ money, and could lead to a lack of workforce continuity, with the potential to undermine safe care.A Regional Group has been established to co-ordinate the improvement of staff utilisation, with the aim of reducing unsustainable agency and locum spend. Significant progress has already been made towards the cessation of ‘Off-Framework’ agency use with expenditure falling from £134m in 22/23 to £31m in 23/24 across Nursing & Midwifery and Social Workers. For these two staff groups, the continued progress being made is projected to yield an overall reduction in agency expenditure in 2024/25 compared to the base year (2022/23), of £48m.In addressing the Medical & Dental locum costs work is already underway including implementation of a new Medical & Dental framework; Medical & Dental workforce planning; communication; development of regional guidance for the utilisation and monitoring of locums; and an uplift in the rates payable under the current HSC e-locum service.I very much welcome the ongoing progress that has been made across HSC in relation to reducing agency usage especially at a time when the health service is facing a period of unprecedented budgetary pressures. Reducing agency spend is important and not just because of the costs involved; HSC staff have been clear that excessive agency use is unfair and impacts on morale and more importantly, having consistent clinical teams also enhances patient safety.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to outline when the criteria for applying for an exclusion from the rating of empty domestic properties was last reviewed.
answer_text: The rating of empty homes policy was introduced in October 2011.Amendments have been made to the policy since then. For example in 2013 exclusions were introduced for the Purchase of Evacuated Dwellings. In 2013 and 2014 adjustments to developer exclusions were made and changes were made in 2017 to allow for eligible flooded properties to be granted exclusions. The policy for exclusions for Rating of Empty Homes in the round will be reviewed as part of the scheduled work on the Strategic Review of Rating – that work will see every rates support measure reviewed. To be clear review does not mean removal. This is about ensuring the support we have in place is achieving the desired policy outcome and using the resources we have to best meet the needs of our citizens and businesses.
