question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether Northern Ireland has security of supply in terms of import facilities for oil and gas.
answer_text: The North is served by three oil terminals - Puma and Valero in Belfast, and LSS in Derry. All three terminals are supplied by individual access points. Oil and Petroleum are sourced through international trading markets using a diverse range of providers. Officials from my Department regularly engage with the oil industry and on an annual basis undertake winter preparedness checks to assess fuel stocks and availability.Natural gas is supplied through the SNIP gas pipeline from Scotland.  The North also has gas interconnection with the South through the South-North gas pipeline. This pipeline would be used to supplement gas flow to the North in peak demand events. All of the North’s natural gas is supplied from Britian and my officials regularly engage with counterparts in Britian on gas storage and security of supply. During winter 2024/25, Britian’s gas stocks and storage were extremely healthy.My Department meets regularly with the gas transmission operator, Mutual Energy, and also participates in an annual gas emergency exercise that tests the resilience of the network through the application of emergency procedures and co-ordinated plans.  These exercises are continuously reviewed to ensure best practice procedures.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of the availability of preschool nursery places for 2025-26 in the Carrickfergus area.
answer_text: The Education Authority Pre-School Education Group (EA PEG) is responsible for ensuring that sufficient pre-school provision is available in all areas to provide a funded pre-school education place for every target age child whose parents want it.The pre-school admissions process has two stages and the EA has advised that at this point, 97% of target aged children whose parents applied for a pre-school place have already received an offer of a funded place in a setting of their preference.The EA has advised that there are currently 2,316 funded places available across Northern Ireland to accommodate the 620 children who have not yet received the offer of a funded place in a setting of their parents’ preference. Within the Mid and East Antrim council area 650 funded pre-school places were available for 2025/26 with a total of 584 first preference applications received during Stage One of the admissions process.  The EA has advised that 550 target age children received an offer of a funded place on 3 April (not all first preference).  A further 59 children have yet to receive an offer of a place in a preferred setting.  However, the EA has advised that sufficient pre-school provision remains within the Mid and East Antrim council area with 104 funded places still available.Parents whose children have not yet received the offer of a funded place in one of their preferred settings have already received advice on the next steps and have been invited to submit further preferences from Monday 7 April.  A list of providers with funded places still available has been posted on the EA website.I would encourage parents to engage with the admissions process to the end and provide further preferences when invited to do so in order to maximise the chances of their child being offered a place in one of their preferred settings.  Should additional funded places be required in local areas, the EA will adjust allocations to settings as required.  Parents will be advised of Stage Two application outcomes on 10th June.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline what steps their Department is taking to protect Northern Ireland businesses affected by the recent announcement of a ten per cent tariff on goods entering the United States of America.
answer_text: As international trade is a reserved issue, we have engaged directly with the Prime Minister and with the Secretary of State for Business and Trade, Jonathan Reynolds, to argue the case for local businesses and to ensure that our economy and businesses here are protected as far as possible in both trade negotiations and in terms of any countermeasures. We have similarly engaged with the Irish Government given the impact of any retaliatory measures imposed by the EU may affect local businesses.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any consideration given to the overall availability of preschool places in the context of the Standardisation of the Preschool Education Programme.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) is responsible for securing sufficient pre-school places to provide a funded place for every target aged child whose parents want it. The pre-school admissions process consists of two stages. The EA has advised that 19,861 children (97%) received the offer of a funded pre-school place in a setting of their parents’ preference on 3 April.  The EA has further advised that there are currently 2,209 funded places available to accommodate the 620 children who have not yet received the offer of a funded place in a setting of their parents’ preference.  Parents whose child did not receive the offer of a place on 3 April are strongly encouraged to consider all the remaining places available in their area, list further preferences and engage with the process to increase their chances of securing a funded pre-school education place.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she will review the current means by which planning applications are advertised in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The issue of advertisement of planning applications was considered in the first report of the Review of the Implementation of the Planning Act (RIPA) where the Department committed to undertaking a review of the publicity and advertisement requirements in planning.I can confirm that preliminary scoping work on this review has commenced, including early engagement with local councils and the Planning Appeals Commission.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether consideration can be given to requiring local council planning departments to display site notices at application sites providing the public with (i) brief details of the proposal; (ii) the planning reference number; (iii) the case officer; (iv) the deadline for submitting objections and comments.
answer_text: The issue of advertisement of planning applications was considered in the first report of the Review of the Implementation of the Planning Act (RIPA) where the Department committed to undertaking a review of the publicity and advertisement requirements in planning.I can confirm that preliminary scoping work on this review has commenced, including early engagement with local councils and the Planning Appeals Commission.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether consideration is being given for the approved use of abiraterone as an NHS treatment option for patients (i) newly diagnosed with high risk, non-metastatic, hormone-sensitive prostate cancer; and (ii) whose prostate cancer has relapsed after at least 12 months without treatment.
answer_text: In Northen Ireland and across the UK, abiraterone (Zytiga®) is currently licensed for use for the treatment of newly diagnosed high risk metastatic hormone sensitive prostate cancer (mHSPC) and the treatment of metastatic castration resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC). Use of abiraterone (Zytiga®) as a treatment option for patients newly diagnosed with high risk, non-metastatic, hormone-sensitive prostate cancer, or in whom prostate cancer has relapsed after at least 12 months without treatment, is not included within the terms of the marketing authorisation and as such use for this purpose is considered to be ‘off-label’ and therefore not routinely available in HSC.My Department has a formal link with the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) under which NICE Technology Appraisals are reviewed locally for their legal and policy applicability here, and where found to be applicable, they are endorsed for implementation within Health and Social Care (HSC) organisations. This link has ensured that Northern Ireland has access to up-to-date, independent, professional, evidence-based guidance on the value of health care interventions. Where NICE has recommended the use of abiraterone (Zytiga®) in the treatment of prostate cancer, this medicine is available to eligible patients in Northern Ireland, in line with the current NICE guidelines. Further information on this treatment is available at the following links:https://www.nice.org.uk/guidance/ta259https://www.nice.org.uk/guidance/ta387https://www.nice.org.uk/guidance/ta951In NI, abiraterone (Zytiga®) is not recommended for the treatment of newly diagnosed high-risk hormone-sensitive metastatic prostate cancer in adults, in line with the recommendations set out in NICE TA721 (https://www.nice.org.uk/guidance/ta721)In circumstances where NICE is yet to complete a full appraisal, or does not recommend a particular drug, the Department’s Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) does not routinely commission that therapy for use in Northern Ireland.However, the individual funding request (IFR) process provides a mechanism to consider requests from clinical consultants for treatments for individual patients that fall outside the range of services routinely commissioned. Clinical decisions such as these are a matter for the patient’s specialist consultant, in discussion with the patient or carer. Further information can be found online at Individual Funding Requests (IFRs) - HSC (hscni.net).Should NICE update its recommendations for abiraterone (Zytiga®) for its use in the treatment of prostate cancer, my Department will consider it under the usual process for NICE Technology Appraisals as per the guidance set out in circular HSC (SQSD) 12/22 which is available at:https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/health/doh-hsc-sqsd-12-22.pdf

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail how commitments to active travel can be imbedded in planning policy and development control decisions.
answer_text: Encouraging active travel is a regional strategic objective for transportation and land-use planning, as set out in the Department’s Strategic Planning Policy Statement (the SPPS). The provisions of the SPPS must be taken into account in the preparation of local development plans (LDPs) and in the determination of planning applications. The SPPS requires that LDPs should identify active travel networks and provide a range of infrastructure improvements to increase use of more sustainable modes, providing enhanced priority to pedestrians, cyclists, and public transport. A number of active travel delivery plans exist within my Department, namely the Belfast Cycling Network Delivery Plan, the draft Active Travel Delivery Plan and the Strategic Plan for Greenways.  These plans provide information on existing and future active travel networks to developers, local councils and development control offices.I recognise that the planning system should continue to encourage active travel to help mitigate and adapt to climate change. Last year, my Department undertook a call for evidence (CfE) on a potential focussed review of the SPPS in relation to climate change, including  the subject policy provisions on transportation. The information received is being analysed and will help inform a future decision on whether to commence a formal policy development process and the areas of focus for such a review. Parallel to the preparation of LDPs, and in support of such plans, a suite of Transport Plans is being developed. Transport Plans will reflect local ambitions and priorities and will also shape sustainable transport policies within  councils’ plan documents.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she is committed to achieve the target of allocating 10 per cent of her Department’s overall annual budget to active travel by 2030.
answer_text: Section 22 of the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 stipulates that “The Department for Infrastructure must develop sectoral plans for transport which set a minimum spend on active travel from the overall transport budgets of 10%.”My Department is working on the basis of ramping up overall investment in active travel to meet this requirement by 2030.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of whether the target in the Climate Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 for at least 10 per cent of her Department’s overall annual budget to be allocated to active travel projects by 2030 is legally binding.
answer_text: The Climate Change Act is legally binding. Section 22 of the Act stipulates that “The Department for Infrastructure must develop sectoral plans for transport which set a minimum spend on active travel from the overall transport budgets of 10%.” and my Department is working towards meeting that target by 2030.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on compensation claims from businesses and individuals who were victims of violent attacks and disorder during the summer of 2024.
To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail how her Department promotes and develops entrepreneurship in younger people.
answer_text: Promoting and developing entrepreneurship in younger people is vitally important and aligns with my key priorities, particularly the promotion of regional balance. 		Our Further Education Colleges’ Entrepreneurship Hubs provide advice and guidance, mentoring, signposting to wider assistance, and support for business plan development to their students. My Department also funds FE students to participate in Young Enterprise NI’s Company Programme, promoting an entrepreneurial spirit and culture across further education. 		Through Invest NI, my Department has committed £12,000 to Young Enterprise NI as part of an annual Environmental, Social and Governance (ESG) Business Backer Partnership.  Invest NI, also provide advice and information through www.nibusinessinfo.co.uk. 	Two parts of the website are particularly helpful for young people considering starting their own businesses – My New Business | nibusinessinfo.co.uk and Starting a business | nibusinessinfo.co.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the (i) roads maintenance; and (ii) resurfacing schemes planned for 2025-26 in the East Antrim constituency.
answer_text: My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government.While there are many roads that would benefit from investment, due to limited budget resources, resurfacing work is taken forward through the prioritisation of those deemed most in need for intervention.Details of the resurfacing schemes planned for the 2025/26 financial year will be included in my Department’s Annual Report to Council.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the road adoption process for Trailcock Close, Carrickfergus.
answer_text: My Department is currently enforcing the completion of Trailcock Close and has issued notice to the developer under Article 11 of the Private Streets (NI) Order 1980. As this enforcement action is currently ongoing it is not possible to provide a date for completion of the adoption process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on capital works plans and maintenance upgrades at Eden Primary School, Carrickfergus.
answer_text: I have decided to take a phased approach to the development of the Social Inclusion Strategies, with work currently underway at pace on the Anti-Poverty and Disability Strategies.The timeframe for the development of the Sexual Orientation Strategy will be confirmed soon.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on capital work plans and maintenance upgrades at Greenisland Primary School.
answer_text: Greenisland Primary School was announced in May 2020 to advance in design under the Second Call to the School Enhancement Programme (SEP2).A Technical Feasibility Report has been completed, and options include extensions to the existing school to provide toilets/cloaks and resource areas and internal reconfigurations to provide ancillary accommodation, provision of a secure lobby entrance and pupil changing and hygiene room to the multi-purpose hall. A business case is currently being prepared by the Education Authority (EA).Funding is available to progress SEP projects through the design stages to pre-tender. A decision to release projects to tender for a contractor will be taken on a case-by-case basis, dependent on budget availability at that time.A minor works business case for traffic management and car parking was approved by the EA in May 2024 with a total project value of £319,981.68. A planning application for this scheme was submitted on 26 February 2025.I will continue to make the case for additional funding for education through the Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the amount paid to NSL Marston Holdings as part of its parking enforcement contract, broken down year since the contract was awarded.
answer_text: Details of the annual cost of my Department’s current parking enforcement contract is set out in the table below from the formation of the Department for Infrastructure in 2016.  Costs are determined by the level of service delivered in each year.  Financial Year£'m2016-177.002017-187.002018-197.402019-207.302020-218.102021-227.802022-238.502023-248.80

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to set out the tender process for contracting out (i) on-street; and (ii) off-street car parking enforcement.
answer_text: The current parking enforcement contract for on and off street car parking was awarded via a competitive tendering process, undertaken by my Department, in 2012. The competition was managed in accordance with the Public Contracts Regulations 2006, following the Competitive Dialogue Procedures (Reg 18). The Competitive Dialogue process allowed my Department to discuss our requirements with the bidders, prior to the awarding of the contract.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail how his Department supports local enterprise agencies across Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Local Enterprise Agencies (LEAs) are an important stakeholder in the enterprise eco-system in the north. Invest NI's Regional Business Group maintains close contact with the LEAs, referring businesses to them, and encouraging them to do likewise.  It is crucial that LEAs, Invest NI, InterTradeIreland, local Councils and others work in partnership to deliver joined-up support for local businesses. To that end, I have tasked Invest NI with facilitating greater co-operation between organisations at the local level through a revitalized regional office network. I am also seeking to enhance partnership working by supporting councils to create Local Economic Partnerships (LEPs).  The LEAs are mandatory partners in the LEPs, providing further opportunities for collaboration and codesign, and will have influence on the projects to be funded through the Regional Balance Fund.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on capital work plans and maintenance upgrades at Carrickfergus Grammar School.
answer_text: Carrickfergus Grammar School was announced in May 2020 to advance in design under the second call to the School Enhancement Programme (SEP).  As a controlled school, the Education Authority (EA) is managing the project.A Technical Feasibility Report has been completed for the SEP project, and the options being considered include provision of a Sports Centre and Home Economics accommodation. In January 2025 a structural engineer’s report on the modular unit housing the existing Home Economic facilities recommended that the unit be condemned and replaced. The EA is currently progressing a project to meet the immediate critical accommodation needs for delivery of the Home Economics curriculum. Options for a permanent solution to replace the Home Economics accommodation are also being investigated. As this could potentially have an impact on the scope of the SEP project, the business case for the SEP project will be produced following confirmation of the preferred option for the replacement Home Economics accommodation.A minor works scheme to provide a pedestrian footpath to the sports pitches is currently at Feasibility Report Stage, with a business case to be prepared. The roofs to the mobile accommodation units are currently being reviewed for remedial action and a lift in the assembly hall is pending the delivery of specialist parts for repairs to be completed.The third and final phase of window upgrades at the school is currently ongoing, with windows currently being manufactured for installation. These works are due to complete by 31 March 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline any changes that have been made to the Literacy Model to support pupils with literacy difficulties, in the 2024-2025 academic year.

To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he has received any advice from Medicines Regulatory Group in relation to the safety and efficacy of the drug Librela for use as a veterinary medicine.
answer_text: Following the coming into force on 12 April 2024 of the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024, veterinary medicines are now subject to the direction and control of Baroness Hayman of Ullock, Defra Parliamentary Under-Secretary of State.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he has received any advice from Medicines Regulatory Group in relation to the safety and efficacy of the drug Librela for use as a veterinary medicine.
answer_text: I have not received any advice from the Medicines Regulatory Group within my Department in relation to the safety and efficacy of the drug Librela for use as a veterinary medicine. Such matters are not within the purview of the Medicines Regulatory Group. Veterinary medicines are regulated on a UK-wide basis by the Veterinary Medicines Directorate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health the detail the relationship between the Medicines Regulatory Group and the Veterinary Medicines Directorate in terms of regulating veterinary medicine in Northern Ireland.
To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the number of days since 1 April 2019 that energy from renewable sources has fallen under 40 per cent of energy used In Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Given the close relationship between the two questions from John Stewart MLA (AQW 20374 and AQW 23075), the answers have been included as one response, below.		The percentage of metered electricity consumption generated from metered renewable sources is published by the Department in the Electricity Consumption and Renewable Generation quarterly statistical report. The underpinning data is provided to the Department monthly and therefore not held in terms of days.		For the 66 months between April 2019 and September 2024 inclusive, the proportion of metered electricity consumption from metered renewable sources was: below 40% for 20 months; between 40% and 60% for 41 months; and above 60% for five months.		The annual average has remained above 40% each year since 2019.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail his Department’s position on whether Battery Energy Storage Systems are strategically required to stabilise the electricity grid in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Battery Energy Storage Systems, like many other technologies, have a role to play in facilitating the just transition to a stable and resilient decarbonised electricity network in the North.My department is working with key stakeholders in the energy industry in respect of all technologies including battery storage.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the number of constraint payments that have been made to (i) wind; and (ii) solar energy operators to curtail generation when electricity supply exceeds demand since 2019.
answer_text: SONI (System Operator for Northern Ireland) advises that, at present in the Single Electricity Market, there are no payments made to any type of generator whose output is reduced when supply exceeds demand.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the number of days since 1 April 2019 that energy from renewable sources has been over 60 per cent of energy used In Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Given the close relationship between the two questions from John Stewart MLA (AQW 20374 and AQW 23075), the answers have been included as one response, below.		The percentage of metered electricity consumption generated from metered renewable sources is published by the Department in the Electricity Consumption and Renewable Generation quarterly statistical report. The underpinning data is provided to the Department monthly and therefore not held in terms of days.		For the 66 months between April 2019 and September 2024 inclusive, the proportion of metered electricity consumption from metered renewable sources was: below 40% for 20 months; between 40% and 60% for 41 months; and above 60% for five months.		The annual average has remained above 40% each year since 2019.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any progress made in commissioning a research exercise into future service provision for both childhood and adult attention deficit hyperactivity disorder.
answer_text: My Department is in the process of commissioning a research exercise with a view to bringing forward recommendations to inform a commissioned ADHD service, subject to future budget availability. The Terms of Reference for an ADHD research exercise have now been finalised and are with the Leadership Centre to identify a suitable individual or individuals to take it forward. It is hoped, therefore, that researchers with the requisite skills, knowledge and experience will be appointed shortly. This research, which would cover both child and adult ADHD, will aim to determine the level of demand for a commissioned service, as well as recommendations for what such a service might look like, including potential implementation and staffing costs.In line with the usual process for commissioning of services, any decision to commission adult ADHD services in the future will be based on an assessment of the level of demand for services, and in the context of future budget availability.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether the pilot project to address adult attention deficit hyperactivity disorder treatment in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust, launched in January 2024, has been completed and assessed.
answer_text: The Adult ADHD service provided by NHSCT remains in a pilot phase and the ongoing provision of the pilot service remains under review.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any policy development that has arisen in the area of residents-only parking consequent to the residents-only parking pilot scheme on the Rugby Road and College Park Avenue area of South Belfast since 2018.
answer_text: My Department has recently completed a review of residents’ parking schemes and the report is available on the Department’s website: https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/residents-parking-review-report. The findings from the review will be used to establish the approach to delivering such schemes going forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any plans he has to roll-out residents-only parking schemes.
answer_text: My Department has recently completed a review of residents’ parking schemes and the report is available on the Department’s website: https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/residents-parking-review-report  Going forward my Department will continue to develop the schemes already commenced such as in the Bogside in Derry and Iveagh area of Belfast, and will give consideration to schemes to address the emerging pressures with on-street parking in the vicinity of the new Ulster University campus in Belfast and the Magee campus.  The development of other schemes will be in line with the local parking strategies which will support the new suite of Transport Plans which are currently in development.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of residential areas that have been assessed for their suitability as residents-only parking zones since 2018.
answer_text: My Department has recently completed a review of residents’ parking schemes and the report is available on the Department’s website: https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/residents-parking-review-report   The report includes information on the other schemes that were assessed and taken forward, and it sets out those which are still being progressed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any assessment that has been made of the residents-only parking pilot scheme on the Rugby Road and College Park Avenue area of South Belfast.
answer_text: My Department recently completed a review of residents’ parking schemes which considered the Rugby Road scheme as well as our approach to residents’ parking in general.  The review report is publicly available on the Department’s website: https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/residents-parking-review-report

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether he will consider reviewing the household thresholds for Educational Maintenance Allowance payments.
answer_text: Question 12 – AQO 1224/22-27I recognise the importance of removing barriers to ensure that everyone can reach their potential and to provide support for those who need it to continue in post-compulsory education or training.The rate of Educational Maintenance Allowance has operated unchanged since academic year 2013/2014, including the thresholds at which people are eligible. The Educational Maintenance Allowance spans my Department and the Department of Education - around 60% of recipients are in school, with 40% of recipients attending a Further Education College.The Education Minister and I have agreed to take forward a joint programme of work drawing from the 14-19 framework and starting with careers as a priority. Support for learners - including EMA, will be considered as part of a future work programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of landfill capacity that is currently available.
answer_text: The most recent NIEA collated figures estimate non-hazardous landfill capacity at approximately 5 million cubic meters. Inert capacity is estimated to be approximately 7 million cubic meters.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to the NI Audit Office Review of Waste Management in Northern Ireland report of July 2024, to detail any steps being taken to fill gaps in information in relation to landfill capacity.
answer_text: NIEA track landfill capacities and landfill waste inputs and outputs. DAERA are assessing projected waste arisings and waste infrastructure for the coming decades.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to the NI Audit Office Review of Waste Management in Northern Ireland report of July 2024, whether he is taking steps to bring in a statutory requirement to track landfill capacities.
answer_text: The Landfill Regulations (NI) 2003 place a requirement on the operator to monitor the remaining capacity still available at their landfill site. The remaining capacity figures are submitted to NIEA within the annual reporting mechanism for the landfill sites. This information is collated and retained by the NIEA and therefore I have not identified a need for an additional statutory duty to be introduced.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education when education support staff can expect to receive a payment arising from the Education Authority’s Pay and Grading Review Agreement.
To ask the Minister of Education to detail any changes in the Education Authority Literacy Service that have been made as a result of the End to End Review of Special Educational Needs.
answer_text: My department is concluding its End to End Review of Special Educational Needs (SEN).  No changes have been made to the Education Authority’s (EA) Literacy Service as a result of the review at this point.The EA’s current Pupil Support Services, of which the Literacy Service is one, is moving to a new ‘Local Integrated Teams’ model in 2025. Term one of 2024/25 is a transitionary term, during which the EA has made a number of changes to services as it prepares to go live with the new model.  The EA advised all schools of the changes and arrangements for accessing support during this transitionary period in early September.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the changes in delivery of special educational needs support in stage 2 of the new draft code of practice.
answer_text: The EA has advised that:If a child or young person is at Stage 2 of the current draft Code of Practice, their school or preschool (setting) can draw on ‘external supports’ to assist in addressing that child’s special educational needs. In the majority of cases, that external support is currently provided by one of the EA’s ten specialist support services. Up until the 2023/24 academic year, these specialist services were operating largely in isolation from one another. In addition, in the majority of cases a child or young person could only be referred to these services if a Stage 2 assessment had first been completed by the EA’s Educational Psychology Service (EPS).Since 2022, the EA has been working to transition away from the above model towards a network of local support teams where all of the EA’s various SEN specialists can work together to tailor support around the individual needs of the child or young person and their setting. Removing the requirement for prior EPS assessment would also allow that support to be provided earlier.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what plans he has to address the increasing number of unadopted developments and roads.
answer_text: Responsibility for the provision of infrastructure in new developments to an adoptable standard lies with the Developer.My Department has implemented the vast majority of recommendations of the Regional Development Committee Inquiry into Unadopted Roads in Northern Ireland, which sought to reduce the risk of new developments remaining unadopted.In 2023 my Department also increased bond calculation rates to ensure that sufficient funding was available to complete outstanding works should a developer default, which further reduces the risk of new developments remaining unadopted.Officials in my department are also considering changes to Private Streets policy that may allow more resource to be allocated to enforcement actions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 7981/22-27 and AQW 7979/22-27, to detail any consideration being given to following the Scottish model of dental payment reform to ensure accessibility to and sustainability of general dentistry services.
answer_text: My officials have been closely monitoring the impact of the dental payment reform introduced in Scotland in November 2023. Unfortunately, the data required to fully analyse the suitability of adopting the same approach in Norther Ireland is not available.High level estimates of the cost to Northern Ireland of adoption of a similar model suggest that it would require a substantial increase to the dental budget, therefore any consideration must take into account the returns on such an investment in terms of increased access and patient and practitioner satisfaction. Further consideration will be given as more information becomes available.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 7981/22-27, for an update on the Dental Access Scheme to provide appointments for unregistered patients who require emergency dental treatment during the working week.
answer_text: A Northern Ireland-wide Dental Access Scheme (DAS) commenced on 1st August 2024.  This new scheme aims to improve access (during the working week) for unregistered patients experiencing an emergency, urgent, or pressing dental condition.  There are currently 29 practices participating in the scheme.  Details of the scheme (including participating practices) can be viewed at:  https://online.hscni.net/dental-access-scheme/ In the first seven weeks 2316 patients were treated under the scheme (outlined below) with 38% of the patients presenting with an urgent dental condition and 62% with a pressing dental condition. 3.1% of the patients were refugees or asylum seekers.WeekNumber of Patients1164238933834334531163717364TOTAL2316

answer_text: A person is eligible for free dental treatment in Northern Ireland if they are:aged under 18aged 18 and in full-time educationpregnant, or have had a child, within the 12 months before treatment startsa hospital inpatient and the treatment is carried out by a hospital dentistgetting, or their partner gets Income Support, income-related Employment and Support Allowance, income-based Jobseeker’s Allowance, or Pension Credit Guarantee Creditentitled to, or named on a valid tax credit exemption certificatea war pensioner and need the dental treatment test because of a disability which they get a war pension fora Hospital Dental Service outpatient*a Community Dental Service patient**If the person is a Hospital Dental Service outpatient or a Community Dental Service patient, they may have to pay for dentures and bridges. Advice for patients on the cost of dental treatment can be found on NI Direct at https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/articles/seeing-dentist

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, in light of the Supreme Court's decision in the case of Finch v Surrey County Council, whether updated guidance will be provided to planning decision makers to ensure all Environmental Statements, submitted under the Planning (Environmental Impact Assessment) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2017, will provide an assessment of downstream, scope 3, greenhouse gas emissions.
answer_text: Any planning authority determining an application for planning permission which requires environmental impact assessment (EIA) is required to meet the statutory requirements set out in relevant legislation.  The findings in the Finch case may be relevant to that authority’s decision-making process dependent upon the individual circumstances of the application under consideration. While my Department has produced a number of development management practice notes on key elements of the statutory EIA process these relate to established processes and approaches in the EIA process. I do not intend to provide guidance based on this specific legal case as the weight to be given to the findings in the case, or any other relevant court decision, is a matter of professional planning judgement by any relevant planning authority.I understand that the relevant Whitehall department in Britain may in due course issue its own guidance and this may be of relevance to planning authorities in the North to inform their decision-making.  If and when such information becomes available, my officials will bring it to the attention of our district councils in their role as local planning authorities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any account that is taken in the planning development management system of decarbonisation targets set in the (i) Climate Change Act 2008; (ii) Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022; and (iii) Environment Act 2021.
answer_text: I am committed to delivering against decarbonisation targets but recognise that the Executive as a whole needs to work collectively towards the delivery of net zero.The Planning Act (Northern Ireland) 2011 (2011 Act) is the primary planning legislation for the North of Ireland which provides the basis for the development planning, development management and enforcement systems as well as other planning functions. It is the responsibility of the relevant decision maker to assess each planning application on a case-by-case basis, in accordance with Section 45 of the 2011 Act, regard is to be had to the local development plan so far as material to the application, and to any other material consideration. While there is no legal definition for ‘material considerations’, they are held to include all the fundamental factors involved in land-use planning, including addressing climate change.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether major energy development planning applications that have significant implications for energy security or meeting net-zero greenhouse gas emission targets should be referred by him to the Executive for final decision, as a cross-cutting policy issue.
answer_text: Each planning application put before me is assessed on a case-by-case basis, having regard to material planning considerations and taking account of relevant legislation and caselaw.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether consideration has been given to the new Environmental Improvement Plan for Northern Ireland, arising from the Environment Act 2021, in terms of the planning policy hierarchy.
answer_text: I recognise that the planning system plays an important role in helping to deliver improvements in the quality of our environment. The Environmental Improvement Plan states that “the Department for Infrastructure and local planning authorities will work co-operatively with DAERA, in its role as a statutory consultee in the planning system, to inform Local Development Plans and the determination of planning applications.” You will be aware that The Planning Act (Northern Ireland) 2011 established a ‘plan-led’ planning system whereby primacy is given to the plan in the determination of planning applications unless material considerations indicate otherwise. Whilst my Department is responsible for formulating and coordinating regional planning policy, Councils are responsible for the preparation of local policies through their Local Development Plans. I have no plans at this time to bring forward fresh legislative change in this regard. Under the two-tier planning system, the relevance and weight to be attached to the Local Development Plan, regional planning policy and all other material planning considerations is ultimately a matter of planning judgement for the relevant planning authority to decide on a case-by-case basis.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the current (i) legislation; (ii) statutory instruments; and (ii) regulations considered material in planning development management decisions.
answer_text: The Planning Act (N.I.) 2011 (2011 Act) is the primary planning legislation for the North of Ireland which provides the basis for the development planning, development management and enforcement systems as well as other planning functions. Under the 2011 Act, local councils have the primary responsibility for the implementation of the key planning functions including development management (excluding regionally significant applications). Section 23 of the 2011 Act defines the meaning of development, while section 24 specifies that planning permission is required for the carrying out of any development of land. Sections 45 to 57 of the 2011 Act are powers specific to the determination of planning applications. Other relevant subordinate legislation includes: The Planning (General Development Procedure) Order (Northern Ireland) 2015;The Planning (Development Management) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2015; andThe Planning (Environmental Impact Assessment) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2015.In making a determination under the 2011 Act, regard is also to be had to the local development plan so far as material to the application, and to any other material consideration. While there is no legal definition for ‘material considerations’, they are held to include all the fundamental factors involved in land-use planning. Essentially a material consideration is one which is relevant to making a planning decision as to whether to grant or refuse an application for planning permission. Material considerations will vary depending on the specific circumstances of each case.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to AQW 9953/22-27, to detail the number of businesses that have applied for a local producer’s licence, broken down by (i) distilleries; (ii) breweries; and (iii) cideries.
answer_text: A total of twenty-one businesses have applied for a local producer’s licence.  Of these (i) fourteen are involved in the distillation or blending of alcoholic spirits; (ii) six are breweries; and (iii) one produces cider.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to AQW 9953/22-27, to detail the number of local breweries that have applied for a local producer’s licence that have (i) been granted, including a suitability order for on-trade consumption; (ii) been granted, without a suitability order for on-trade consumption; and (iii) been rejected.
answer_text: A total of six breweries have applied for a local producer’s licence as provided for in the Licensing and Registration of Clubs (Amendment) Act (Northern Ireland) 2021.  Of these, (i) one has been granted a suitability order for on-trade consumption; and (ii) four have been granted without a suitability order for on-trade consumption. (iii) One application for a local producer’s licence on behalf of a brewery was rejected.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any engagement his Department has had with the Office of Rail and Road in relation to the new Belfast Grand Central Station.
answer_text: The Department is responsible for ensuring NI Railways’ regulatory compliance with the rail safety and interoperability regulations and grants authority for new infrastructure to enter into service. The Office of Road and Rail is assisting the Department to meet its statutory obligations with respect to rail safety and interoperability by providing technical advice and support in assessing Translink’s application for authority to enter Belfast Grand Central Station into service.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on apprenticeship places.
answer_text: Question 14 – AQO 942/22-27Over 6,600 people started a Level 2 or Level 3 apprenticeship in the 2023/24 academic year to end April 2024, a 30% increase on 2022/23.  Since Higher Level Apprenticeships began in 2017, there has been a 244 per cent increase in starts, from a base of 324 to 1,115 for the most recently published statistics.  These higher level apprentices undertake training across a wide range of sectors in over 70 qualifications ranging from foundation degree level through to masters.  In addition, all age apprenticeships now provide employers with fully funded training for even more people to learn new skills and gain a recognised qualification.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what engagement he has had with Enterprise Northern Ireland and the local enterprise agency network.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the process by which his Department values land and property when vesting for major roads schemes.
answer_text: My Department has powers under Articles 110 to 113 of the Roads (NI) Order 1993  (the 1993 Order) to acquire land compulsorily, or by agreement, for any purpose in connection with the performance of any of its functions under the 1993 Order. In the context of major road schemes, my Department's powers include, constructing new roads; bridges, tunnels and related structures; extending and improving or reorganising the trunk road network; abandoning and stopping-up of existing roads; and stopping-up of private accesses onto roads. The exercise of any of these functions may require my Department to acquire land compulsory by way of a vesting order. The procedure for making a vesting order is the same for all government departments and is contained in Schedule 6 of the Local Government (NI) Act 1972, as amended by Schedule 7 of the Roads (NI) Order 1993All compensation matters relating to major road schemes are dealt with by the Department of Finance's Land and Property Services (LPS). LPS have published their own guidance which is available at:https://www.finance-ni.gov.uk/publications/compulsory-purchase-and-compensation-guides; It is important to note that my Department does not negotiate directly with landowners on matters of compensation. These issues are dealt with by LPS which acts on behalf of DfI. LPS considers claims for compensation in accordance with the appropriate legislation, and land and property owners may employ a professional agent to act on their behalf in negotiations with LPS on all aspects of compensation. My Department will pay reasonable agent’s fees for costs incurred in relation to the land acquired for major road schemes. Such costs must be agreed by LPS. These arrangements normally result in the compensation being agreed between LPS and the landowner’s agent. In very exceptional circumstances, where agreement cannot be reached the landowner has recourse to refer their case to the Lands Tribunal for determination. I would also refer the Member to my Department's booklet “Guidelines for the Acquisition of Land/Property for Major Road Development in Northern Ireland” https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/guidelines-acquisition-landproperty-major-road-development-northern-ireland.  With regard to major road schemes, my Department has made 4 vesting orders in 2017, none in 2018, 2019 or 2020, one in 2021, and none in 2022, 2023 and to date, none in 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the application by Carrickfergus Grammar School for a new synthetic sports pitch to replace their old shale hockey pitch.
answer_text: In 2022 a shale hockey pitch at Carrickfergus Grammar School was replaced with a synthetic pitch.  As a result of the extremely constrained capital budget position, all available investment has been necessarily directed to meet contractual commitments, provide accommodation for a significantly increased number of pupils with Special Educational Needs and deliver inescapable health and safety works. There are therefore no current plans to provide a second synthetic pitch at the school. It is my intention to launch a series of targeted smaller scale investment programmes to support and enhance the delivery of the curriculum in schools. One potential programme will be for sports facilities, and this could provide an opportunity for the school to enhance its sports provision. Further detail on this will be provided in due course.
