question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on his Department's projects under the Belfast Region City Deal.
answer_text: The Belfast Region City Deal brings together £700m of NI Executive and UK Government investment with another £300m expected from delivery partners and the private sector. This investment will strengthen the region’s offer in multiple growth sectors and create up to twenty thousand jobs. My officials are working collaboratively with council partners to support the development and delivery of three tourism and regeneration projects under the Deal.Each of these projects are at various stages within the design and planning phase, but I am very pleased to report that excellent progress has been made across all three.The Newry City Centre Project is an ambitious regeneration plan to create a new Civic Hub and Theatre and Conference Centre in the heart of the city. The project aims to provide six hundred jobs in both construction and through the life of the asset, and a GVA impact circa £122.6m over the thirty-year period. Construction is due to commence on the Newry project in early 2025, subject to planning approval. The Bangor Waterfront Project will regenerate over two miles of the Bangor waterfront, connecting the city to the sea through a range of world-class visitor and leisure attractions. It aims to create five hundred jobs and increase visitor numbers by one-hundred thousand each year, increasing GVA by £110m, as well as providing environmental and health and wellbeing benefits. Two elements of the project have design teams appointed, with further appointments expected in the coming months. The Carrickfergus Project will see significant investment in the Castle and Town Walls, as well as the creation of a new Visitor and Community Hub and regeneration of the town centre. The project aims to increase economic activity in the town centre and boost tourism providing an increase of £36.9m from visitor spend alone. McAdam design is developing detailed plans, with stakeholder and community engagement underway.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline what priority is given to amalgamate schools in the Department’s programme for new build projects.
answer_text: A Protocol is agreed and published in advance of a call being made for major capital works projects.  The Protocol details how applications lodged will be assessed and provides the scoring available for each of the relevant assessment categories.  For the most recent call for projects which was published in December 2021, ten points were awarded for amalgamated schools.  The total points available were 120. As part of the development of a capital investment strategy, my officials are considering how to ensure that the capital planning process supports the implementation of Area Planning in a dynamic and responsive manner.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the legislation and regulations that pertain to equine dentistry.
answer_text: All diagnostic and treatment procedures in a horse’s mouth are considered to be acts of veterinary surgery and fall under the Veterinary Surgeons Act 1966.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the action that can be taken to ensure that veterinary practitioners who undertake equine dental procedures are suitably qualified and registered.
answer_text: It is my understanding that in 2009, the Royal College of Veterinary Surgeons reached a consensus with the British Equine Veterinary Association (BEVA), the British Veterinary Dental Association (BVDA) and the British Association of Equine Dental Technicians (BAEDT) that a number of proscribed procedures could be safely delegated to suitably qualified equine dental technicians.The training and registration of individuals performing acts of equine dentistry remains a matter for these organisations.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will consider amending the Planning (Control of Advertisements) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2015 to prohibit the display of election posters on lampposts and other street furniture.
answer_text: I consider there to be adequate legislative planning provisions within section 130 of the Planning Act (Northern Ireland) 2011 and the Planning (Control of Advertisements) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2015, together with the provisions of the Roads (NI) Order 1993, to regulate and control the display of election posters on lampposts and street furniture.  As such, I have no plans to amend the legislation at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will consider amending Article 87 of the Roads (Northern Ireland) Order 1993 to include election posters in the definition of an unauthorised sign or advertisement on lampposts and other street furniture.
answer_text: There are no current plans to amend Article 87 of the Roads Order.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will consider adding the Health and Safety Executive for Northern Ireland and local Northern Ireland Fire and Rescue Service stations as statutory consultees on applications for industrial Battery Energy Storage Systems.
answer_text: While there are no current plans to amend the list of statutory consultees a planning authority may, at their discretion, and subject to their professional judgment consult either the Health and Safety Executive for Northern Ireland or Northern Ireland Fire and Rescue Service, or indeed any other body, on an application for planning permission.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what consideration is being given to issuing guidance on minimum distances from residential properties in relation to planning applications for lithium-ion Battery Energy Storage Systems.
answer_text: Through an existing framework which includes planning policies; local development plans; guidance; environmental impact assessments; case law; pre-application community consultation; pre-application discussions; consultation with expert bodies and consideration at planning committee meetings I am content that the risks associated with these developments and any separation distances from residential properties can be addressed at present.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether the Control of Major Accident Hazards Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2000 apply to Battery Energy Storage Systems operators.
answer_text: The Control of Major Accident Hazards (COMAH) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2015 are enforced by a Competent Authority consisting of the Health and Safety Executive Northern Ireland (HSENI) and the Northern Ireland Environment Agency (NIEA) acting jointly. HSENI advises on risks to people and the NIEA leads on risks to the environment. A battery is considered an ‘article’ and not a dangerous substance. As batteries are not classified as dangerous substances under the Classification, Labelling and Packaging of Chemicals (Amendment) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2015 (CLP), batteries would not of their own accord bring a BESS facility within the scope of COMAH.The definition (loss of control) is not intended to bring into scope premises which do not manufacture, use or store dangerous substances, solely because of dangerous substances being generated in an accident.Furthermore, the scope of COMAH is considered for BESS facilities due to dangerous substances generated through loss of control if other dangerous substances are already present. Therefore, each BESS facility needs to be considered individually to ensure appropriate requirements are applied.The above approach is consistent with other enforcement authorities in Britain and the South of Ireland.Operators of BESS facilities are required to comply with the Health and Safety at Work (NI) Order 1978, and other relevant statutory provisions. The operator has a duty to implement measures to control risks and mitigate consequences, so far as is reasonably practicable.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the Tackling Paramilitarism, Criminality and Organised Crime programme.
answer_text: The Tackling Paramilitarism, Criminality and Organised Crime Programme is a Department of Justice led cross Executive Programme. The Programme is now in its ninth year of delivery. Over one hundred interventions are making a measurable difference to people and communities. As a result of these interventions, paramilitary style assaults, intimidations and trauma levels are down while arrests, charges and seizures are up. This cross Executive Programme collectively is making a difference to the lives of many thousands of people in our communities. TEO has responsibility for the Communities in Transition element of the project which has delivered community resilience interventions through multiple community and statutory partners, under seven themes, across eight designated areas in Northern Ireland, with a total investment of over £19 million. If we are to eradicate paramilitarism and organised crime here we must continue to support a robust cross Executive multi-agency approach.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline what plans he has to review the Education Authority’s procurement policies.
answer_text: Firstly, it is important to recognise that the Education Authority is designated as education’s Centre of Procurement Expertise (CoPE).  This means the EA is directly responsible for the procurement of goods and services on behalf of schools. As education’s CoPE, the EA is responsible for ensuring that effective procurement practice is embedded across the education sector to provide best value for money. Controlled and maintained schools are therefore required to comply with the EA’s purchasing and contracting procedures for the procurement of any works, equipment, goods or services, using EA contracts where they exist.All grant funded schools are also required to operate within the procurement legal framework and to adhere to Northern Ireland Public Procurement Policy and the relevant Northern Ireland Procurement Guidance Notes.  Controlled and maintained schools have the autonomy to procure any non-contracted items via the EA’s i-Procurement system for goods or services not exceeding £5,000.  For these procurements, two quotations are required to be provided by a school to demonstrate value-for-money has been achieved.  Petty cash may also be used by schools to purchase low value or incidental items that cannot be procured through the i-Procurement system. For contracted procurements the EA works to ensure best value for money by conducting competitive tender exercises, in compliance with both legislation and Northern Ireland Public Procurement Policy.  Frameworks and contracts are awarded to the bidder and / or bidders that provide the best value for money with respect to both quality and cost.  It is important to note that in the context of Northern Ireland Public Procurement Policy, lowest cost alone does not necessarily represent best value for money.  In terms of recent developments, the EA has been developing a new procurement strategy for its new maintenance and minor works contracts.  These will be based on defined geographical areas and will allow schools greater freedom to engage locally approved suppliers.  In addition, the EA has undertaken a detailed review of schools’ tenant/ landlord responsibilities. As a result, the EA plans to introduce new arrangements, with the aim of providing greater flexibility for the delivery of Local Management of Schools (LMS) low-value maintenance works and cost certainty across the EA schools’ estate. The EA will also explore, where appropriate, the application of this type of delivery model more widely across the range of goods and services which it currently procures on behalf of schools.Finally, the Minister of Finance has commissioned a review of Public Procurement, including the Procurement Board, in line with recommendations made by the Northern Ireland Audit Office in its April 2023 Review of Public Procurement.  Both my Department and the EA look forward to contributing to this review.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the risk of avian flu.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the annual value of the contract for on-street parking enforcement.
answer_text: My Department’s parking enforcement contract is not a fixed cost contract.  Costs are service driven which result in fluctuating annual costs. The average annual cost of the Parking Enforcement contract over the last 3 years is £8.48m. Costs also include an element of off-street enforcement for local councils which ended on 1 April 2024 when local councils took responsibility for car park enforcement in their areas.The current parking enforcement contract is due to end on 31 October 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail what policy changes have been implemented in relation to vacant property rating and empty premises relief since the Review of Northern Ireland’s Non-Domestic Rating System in 2015-16.
answer_text: Since that work in 2015-16, the Back in Business scheme to incentivise the use of long-term vacant property was reintroduced in an enhanced form in 2022. The scheme was enhanced to uplift the 12 month rate relief to offer businesses 24  months rate relief at 50%. The scheme lapsed in 2023 due to the absence of an Assembly, but I have now agreed legislation to restore it. This will be debated in the Assembly later this month.A policy change was also introduced in 2017, to provide an exemption from unoccupied rates for domestic property where the occupier has qualified for assistance in the event of flooding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how many Penalty Charge Notices were issued in East Antrim in the 2023-24 financial year.
answer_text: Quantities of Penalty Charge Notices (PCNs) issued in East Antrim in 2023-24 for On-street, Off-street and bus lane contraventions can be found at the following link https://www.opendatani.gov.uk/dataset/penalty-charge-notice-data

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how many on-street Penalty Charge Notices were overturned on appeal in each of the last five financial years.
answer_text: The table below shows the number of on-street Penalty Charge Notices (PCNs) including bus lane contravention overturned between 26th April 2021 and 25th April 2024.DateTotal PCNs cancelled26th April 21 – 31st March 229,0361st April 22 – 31st March 23  10,4411st April 23 – 31st March 24 12,414As per the Department’s Retention and Disposal schedule, older PCN data is not retained.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when the current contract for on-street car parking enforcement expires.
answer_text: My Department’s parking enforcement contract is not a fixed cost contract.  Costs are service driven which result in fluctuating annual costs. The average annual cost of the Parking Enforcement contract over the last 3 years is £8.48m. Costs also include an element of off-street enforcement for local councils which ended on 1 April 2024 when local councils took responsibility for car park enforcement in their areas.The current parking enforcement contract is due to end on 31 October 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance what consideration has been given to extend the provisions of the Small Business Rate Relief Scheme to replicate the business rates relief scheme in England.
answer_text: My Department has already extended the period for Small Business Rate Relief for the 2024/25 rating year, which will be delivering £21.5m of rate support to 29,000 ratepayers with them receiving a reduction of between 20% and 50% on their rate bill. There is a cost in replicating the scheme in England within the confines of our very different taxbase. Scotland have not adopted the English policy position, and likewise Wales who initially matched the provision found this unaffordable and reduced support to 40% for 2024/25. Rates are a devolved matter, and it is up to the Executive to align support to local needs and its priorities in Government. Every pound spent on additional rate relief is either deducted from the amount available for public spending or is paid for by increased bills for other ratepayers. We also have our own generous support schemes for other sectors of the economy that are not available in England, Scotland or Wales such as Industrial Derating which provides over £71.5m of support to 4,400 local manufacturers.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how many (i) Penalty Charge Notices; and (ii) Excess Charge Notices have been issued by (a) his Department’s Parking Enforcement Processing Unit; and (b) NSL Services Group in 2023-24.
answer_text: Excess Charge Notices (ECNs) have not been issued since illegal parking was decriminalised in 2006.  Since 2006 Penalty Charge Notices (PCNs) are issued for parking contraventions and these are issued by Traffic Attendants (TAs) employed by the Department’s service provider, NSL Ltd.  Quantities of Penalty Charge Notices (PCNs) issued in 2023-24 for On-street, Off-street and bus lane contraventions can be found at the following link https://www.opendatani.gov.uk/dataset/penalty-charge-notice-data

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the work of the NI Bureau in Washington.
answer_text: A new Bureau Director for North America was appointed on 5 August, and arrived in Washington DC on 24 August.In the context of the development of a new International Relations Strategy, the Director will prioritise Business Plans for the US and Canada that support delivery of Executive priorities through partnerships; expand existing relationships to embrace next generation leaders; bring opportunities from the US and Canada into NI and export our expertise; ensure NI continues to be a beacon of bipartisan political engagement; and grow dynamic future-facing relationships with the US and Canada that build on the connections forged through the peace process and also reflect the NI of today. This approach will be underpinned by a new Communications Strategy.To support business planning the new Bureau team has already connected with a broad spectrum of stakeholders here and in the US and Canada, including InvestNI, Tourism Ireland and the British and Irish Embassies, and will continue to do so as it seeks to build partnerships that maximise opportunities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the incentives available for domestic consumers to install air source heat pumps, as a way of encouraging energy efficiency and meeting net zero commitments
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Sustainable Energy Programme, NISEP, provides fully supported and part funded schemes in support of the installation of energy efficiency measures including heat pumps, with the majority of funding targeted at lower income customers and details of which can be found on the Energy Saving Trust website.My department is currently preparing a public consultation on a low carbon heat support scheme in line with Action 8 of the Energy Strategy Action Plan for 2023, with a view to publishing in Summer 2024. The outcome of this consultation will help to inform options regarding the design, rules, and guidance of future support for low carbon heating technologies which will include heat pumps. 24.04.03-NISEP-List-of-Schemes-2024-25.pdf (energysavingtrust.org.uk) https://www.economy-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/economy/Energy-Strategy-2023-Action-Plan-Report.pdf

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 9526/22-27, what consideration is being given to replicating the Boiler Upgrade Scheme available in England and Wales to encourage domestic adoption of air source heat pumps.
answer_text: My Officials are in the early stages of developing a new fuel poverty intervention that will replace the current Affordable Warmth Scheme when it ends in March 2026, and are exploring the option of low carbon heating solutions along with other decarbonisation measures. The Department for Economy launched a public consultation on support for low carbon heat on 8 October 2024. The outcome of this consultation will help to inform options regarding the design, rules, and guidance of future support for low carbon heating technologies which will include heat pumps.The Department for Economy is also supporting delivery partners to further promote low carbon heating technologies through the Northern Ireland Sustainable Energy Programme (NISEP) providing grants for air source heat pumps.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline what learning and skills services are available for prisoners prior to their release.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Prison Service continue to prioritise rehabilitation by supporting and challenging those in our care to change and address their offending behaviour while in custody.   Our focus remains on nine resettlement pathways, based on socio-economic factors which are shown to have an impact on reoffending.  One of those pathways is themed on Education Training & Employment.   Working with our education partner, Belfast Metropolitan College we offer a wide and progressive curriculum designed to meet the needs of individuals, employers and the organisation. The curriculum includes accredited training from Entry Level 3 to Level 3 in essential educational skills such as numeracy and literacy; vocational skills ranging from barbering to bricklaying; and employability skills, including soft skills, CV building and interviewing techniques.  The curriculum also takes account of the unique needs of specific groups including women, older people, foreign nationals, those serving short sentences and those with special education needs.   By providing an appropriate offer we aim to give individuals the opportunity to develop skills and attain qualifications that will help give them the best chance to secure gainful employment on release.    Learning and skills training is accessible to the whole population, including remands and separated prisoners.  All prisoners are required to undertake an educational assessment prior to engaging in learning and skills.  However, many of the remand population refuse to engage in assessments, in the belief that they may be released shortly.   In addition to the core curriculum all persons in custody have access to higher education, currently offered through the Open University.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what investigations have taken place into speed reduction measures in the Edenvale area of Steeple Road, Antrim.
answer_text: Staff from the Ballymena Traffic Management Office met with PSNI Roads Policing Unit and have carried out a preliminary assessment of Steeple Road at Edenvale Townland. They are currently awaiting some supplementary information to ascertain if any engineering interventions are appropriate. One of the best ways to ensure the safety of our roads and fulfil our long term goal of eliminating death and serious injury on roads by 2050 is by changing driver behaviour. If all of us who are drivers do not change our attitudes towards speeding and careless driving, our death toll, which has increased in the past year, will continue to increase.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the implementation of signage at the crossroads with Lislunnan Road and Parkgate Road, Antrim.
answer_text: This work is currently being organised by staff from the Ballymena Traffic Management office.The signs have been procured, awaiting the erection work  is to be programmed by our Small Scale Contractor in the Antrim and Newtownabbey Area.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he plans to extend the compulsory microchipping of dogs, as contained in the Dogs (Amendment) Act (Northern Ireland) 2011, to domestic cats.
answer_text: My Department’s Code of Practice for the Welfare of Cats already recommends that owners ensure their cats are identified, using either a collar or microchip. However, I am aware that from 10 June 2024, the microchipping of pet cats in England will become mandatory.   I am considering the compulsory microchipping of pet cats in the context of all the pressing needs for animal welfare reform in Northern Ireland.  However, ultimately, my capacity to enact legislation will be constrained by the resources available to me and the time left in this shortened mandate.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what plans he has to reopen a Boiler Replacement Scheme for private homeowners on low income.
answer_text: I refer the member to my response to AQW 6960 22-27.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on their St Patrick’s Day visit to Washington DC.
answer_text: We travelled to Washington DC for a series of St Patrick’s Day engagements from 13th to 17th March. It was our first international visit and an important opportunity to consolidate the strong relationship that our region enjoys with the U.S.  This relationship was crucial to our peace process and continues today through investment, knowledge sharing and support.We continue to have access at the highest levels in the U.S., including a meeting with President Biden at the White House to deliver a clear message that government here is back up and running, and it is vital that we seek every opportunity to attract investment, grow our economy and deliver for our citizens.  Our visit focused on showcasing the local economy as a compelling investment opportunity and highlighting the many benefits of doing business here. We were able to deliver these messages at key events such as the Ireland Funds Gala; the NI Bureau breakfast, and the Special Envoy’s event, as well as having a roundtable discussion with the U.S. Chamber of Commerce and business leaders.  Events such as the Speaker of the House of Representatives Luncheon, our fireside chat at Georgetown University and receptions hosted by the Irish and British Ambassadors to the U.S gave us further opportunities to engage with some of the most influential decisions makers so they too can see the potential we have to offer.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of the budget available to the Education Authority for school building maintenance.
answer_text: We have an ageing and dispersed estate of over 1,125 schools with rapidly escalating maintenance needs due to historic under-investment. The consequences of a 15-year backlog in planned maintenance across the schools’ estate are now being felt acutely. Each of these 1000 plus schools is also subject to increasing statutory compliance requirements in regard to disabled access, fire safety, radon, legionella, asbestos and now RAAC – all at significant cost. Every day, Departmental officials are receiving unprecedented emergency requests to replace boilers, heating, electrical and other vital materials such as canteen equipment to provide school dinners, in order to simply keep schools open. The opening allocation for maintenance in 2023-24 was £20.54M, this was completely insufficient this year, illustrated by the additional £13.13M that had to be allocated in year.I want to implement a sustained programme of planned maintenance, but this requires investment.  On this basis, I have submitted a significant bid of £51M for school building maintenance in 2024-25.In 2023-24, we also had to check our schools for RAAC with a programme of surveys across identified schools.  Wall ties in ageing buildings have also presented a risk of collapse and required emergency remedial works to keep children safe and schools open.  I have made separate bids respectively to continue to urgently address these matters.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what consideration his Department has made of the (i) Animal Welfare Committee report, Update to the 2014 FAWC Opinion on the welfare of farmed fish at the time of killing; and (ii) amended and augmented recommendations it contains.
answer_text: My Department has noted the recommendations from the recent Animal WelfareCommittee report on the Welfare of Farmed Fish at the Time of Killing and is currently engaging with colleagues in devolved administrations to determine how the report and its recommendations should be taken forward.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what progress has been made in recruiting a new GP contractor to replace the partners of Larne Medical Practice from 30 April 2024.
answer_text: Over the last number of months, the Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) within the Department of Health (DoH) has been exploring every viable option in an attempt to secure new contractors for Larne Medical Practice.  They are making good progress and will continue to work to ensure the new contract is in place for 01 May 2024As always, it is their priority to ensure there is service continuity for all registered patients.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on NHS dental contract reform.
answer_text: The need for dental reform has been acknowledged for a number of years, however the pandemic followed by the absence of an Executive has hampered progress in this area.I am conscious of the developments in the rest of the UK in recent months, with Scotland, England and Wales adopting diverging models of remuneration. Officials are liaising with their counterparts in each region to monitor the impact of these models and assess the suitability of adopting a similar approach in Northern Ireland.In addition, officials have held a number of engagement events with practitioners from across NI, the results of which will help inform decision making on the future of General Dental Services.I will be discussing these issues with officials as soon as possible, to ensure that General Dental Services is sustainable for both patients and the profession.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what steps can be taken to ensure that NHS dentistry is widely available in East Antrim.
answer_text: A key part of the access issues facing patients are the rates being paid to our dentists for treatment.  The absence of Executive Ministers has meant that it has not been possible to progress the recommended pay uplifts for staffing groups for 2023/24.  This is now my immediate priority. Officials have already been making arrangements in anticipation of funding being made available to ensure that uplifts can be implemented as quickly as possible.I know that an additional £95m has been invested in General Dental Services via the Financial Support Scheme and Rebuilding Support Scheme since April 2020. This came to an end in July 2023 due to the severe financial pressures arising from the Department’s 2023/24 budget settlement.A pilot scheme is currently running to provide appointments to unregistered patients who require emergency dental treatment during the working week. If a patient is not registered with a dentist, requires emergency dental treatment, and has been unable to access an appointment, they can contact their local Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) office for advice. However, no dentists have applied to join the scheme in the Western Local Commissioning District (LDC). Officials are considering other options to support unregistered patients in the west.I am also conscious of the progress that has been made in the rest of the UK on dental reform and I will be discussing these issues with officials as soon as possible, to ensure that the sector here in NI is supported in delivering important care to patients. More immediately, officials have been developing investment proposals to further support General Dental Services and I will consider them in early course.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what plans he has to address the occurrences of road traffic collisions on Collin Road, Ballyclare.
answer_text: Over the past three years, nine road traffic collisions have occurred along the B94 Collin Road. No common causation factors have been identified that could be resolved by safety engineering measures and as such there are no plans for any further collision remedial interventions. However, my department, in conjunction with the PSNI’s Road Policing Unit, will continue to monitor the road to assess any potential road safety improvements.  My department is aware of a serious road traffic collision which occurred recently between the Dunamoy and Sawmill Roads and that the PSNI’s investigation into this is ongoing. Once completed we will review the information and consider any actions as appropriate.One of the best ways to ensure the safety of our roads and fulfil our long term goal of eliminating death and serious injury on roads by 2050 is by changing driver behaviour. If all of us who are drivers do not change our attitudes towards speeding and careless driving, our death toll, which has increased in the past year, will continue to increase.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the work of the Northern Ireland Bureaux.
To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will commission an extraordinary audit of Mid and East Antrim Borough Council, as requested by Larne Business Forum.
answer_text: Councils are separate legal entities and are responsible for their own budgets and district rates.The Local Government Auditor is responsible for auditing the financial statements of councils in accordance with the Local Government (NI) Order 2005 and the Local Government Code of Audit Practice. As part of this audit, the Local Government Auditor will consider the financial resilience of the council.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the (i) legislation; and (ii) regulations that enable the police to seize and retain scramblers and other off-road vehicles which are being misused on public streets.
answer_text: There are a number of powers that can be used by the PSNI for the seizure and retention of scramblers and other off-road vehicles that are being misused on public streets.These include:The Road Traffic (Northern Ireland) Order 1981, Article 180B which allows for the seizure of uninsured vehicles, and the Regulations made under Article 180C of that Order, the Road Traffic (Northern Ireland) Order 1981 (Retention and Disposal of Seized Motor Vehicles) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2008;The Road Traffic Regulation (Northern Ireland) Order 1997, Part VIII, which allows for the removal of vehicles which appear to have been abandoned, and can be used in cases where the rider or driver leaves the scene; andThe Criminal Justice (Northern Ireland) Order 2008, Article 65 which allows for the seizure of vehicles being driven carelessly or inconsiderately and causing or likely to cause alarm, distress or annoyance to members of the public, and the Regulations made under Article 66 of that Order, The Criminal Justice (Northern Ireland) Order 2008 (Retention and Disposal of Seized Motor Vehicles) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2008.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice how many (i) scramblers; and (ii) other off-road vehicles have been seized and retained by the PSNI, in each of the last 5 years.
answer_text: The Department of Justice does not hold information on the numbers of scramblers or other off-road vehicles seized or retained by the PSNI.  The recording of information on vehicles seized and retained by police is a matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Northern Ireland Policing Board.You may, therefore, wish to direct any query relating to the issues raised in the question to the PSNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) whether he is responsible for deciding whether planning application LA02/2018/1145/F is to be adjudicated by his Department rather than Mid and East Antrim Borough Council; and (ii) when he will be making his judgement.
answer_text: Mid and East Antrim Borough Council notified my Department on 08 September 2023, as required by a notification direction previously issued by my Department, of its resolution to approve planning application LA02/2018/1145/F (Cloghan Point Oil Terminal). My Department is currently considering this notification, as well as a number of third party ‘call in’ requests, and when this consideration is complete I will then decide whether or not the application should be referred to my Department for determination.     I am conscious of making this decision as quickly as possible to ensure there are no unnecessary delays in the planning process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what consideration has been given to amend the (i) Road Traffic (Northern Ireland) Order 1995; (ii) Motor Vehicle Testing Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2003; and (iii) Goods Vehicle Testing Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2003, to allow MOTs to be conducted in licenced private garages.
answer_text: MOT testing is conducted in compliance with The Road Traffic (Northern Ireland) Order 1995, the Motor Vehicle Testing Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2003 and the Goods Vehicle Testing Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2003, and this existing legislative framework does not provide for vehicle tests to be conducted at private garages.  I can advise that there are no plans to consider changing this legislation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what legal restrictions are applied to the parking of caravans on public streets in residential areas.
answer_text: Article 91 of The Roads (NI) Order 1993 (1993 No. 3160 (N.I. 15)) provides that any person who without reasonable excuse encamps or maintains an encampment on a road including with a caravan shall be guilty of an offence. Regulation 27 of the Road Vehicles Lighting Regulations (NI) 2000 (SR 200 No.169), sets out requirements regarding the use of front and rear position lamps, rear registration plate lamps, side marker lamps and end-outline marker lamps which apply to vehicles parking on roads during the hours between sunset and sunrise.  This would also apply to caravans.Regulation 119 of the Motor Vehicles (Construction and Use) Regulations (NI) 1999 (SR 1999 No.454), concerns the unnecessary obstruction of a road by a motor vehicle or a trailer.Caravans attached to vehicles and/or displaying a Vehicle Registration Mark (VRM) are classed as motor vehicles and subject to any parking restrictions that may be in force. Where parking restrictions do not apply, they are subject to the same provisions as other motor vehicles and should not be parked so as to cause a danger or obstruction. Article 32 of the Road Traffic (Northern Ireland) Order 1995 refers. The PSNI are responsible for investigating offences under Article 32.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what plans he will put in place to mitigate the impact of the delay in providing two new MOT test centres in (i) Hydebank; and (ii) Mallusk.
answer_text: In 2022-2023, the Driver & Vehicle Agency (DVA) conducted over 1.15 million vehicle tests which is the highest number ever recorded in a year. This increase in capacity has been achieved by adopting a range of measures including the recruitment of additional vehicle examiners, the use overtime to provide cover for leave and offering vehicle test appointments on Sundays and bank holidays, when testing is not normally available.  Disappointingly, the number of vehicles that failed to attend a booked appointment in 2022-2023 was also the highest on record at 50,772.A further vehicle examiner recruitment competition launched on 19 February 2024 to fill staff vacancies across the DVA’s network of test centres.The DVA appreciates that, in some cases, customers may not be able to have their vehicles tested before their current MOT certificate expires and has taken steps to ensure that motorists are still able to use their vehicles when they are unable to secure an MOT appointment before their current MOT expires.  The latest information on MOTs is available on NI Direct at A guide to booking your MOT/ vehicle test and is also included as an advice note with every MOT reminder letter issued to customers.Vehicle test appointments are released on a daily basis, when resources become available, so customers are encouraged to regularly check the booking system as they may be able to change their existing appointment to a different test centre and time if a slot becomes available.  Information on how a customer can change, cancel or view their MOT appointment online is available on NI Direct at change, cancel or view MOT/ vehicle test appointment.The construction of the new test centre at Hydebank, in Belfast, is nearing completion and will open later this year.  The construction contract for a second new test centre at Mallusk has also been awarded and this new test centre is expected to open in 2025.  Once fully operational, these two new centres will provide the capacity to test over 200,000 additional vehicles per annum, which will help the DVA meet the future demand for this service.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether vehicle exhaust emissions continue to be tested as part of the MOT.
answer_text: The Driver and Vehicle Agency (DVA) introduced a diesel smoke test for cars, light and heavy goods vehicles and buses in 2006, in compliance with the roadworthiness directive.  However, for health and safety reasons related to the build-up of fumes in the test halls and in consultation with the Health and Safety Executive, the diesel smoke test for cars and light goods vehicles was suspended in June 2006.The DVA currently conducts a fully compliant diesel smoke test on all heavy goods vehicles, buses and vans over 3,500kgs, and a partial diesel emissions test for cars and light goods vehicles.  This includes a visual inspection of the vehicle’s emissions and a check of the engine Malfunction Indicator Lamp (MIL), which indicates a defect in the vehicle emission control systems.The reintroduction of a diesel smoke test for cars and light goods vehicles under 3,500kgs, will require a substantial capital investment in the vehicle testing estate to create a safe environment for staff and customers, to enable the test to be conducted.  The construction of the first new test centre at Hydebank, in Belfast, is nearing completion and will open later this year. The construction contract for a second new test centre at Mallusk has also been awarded and this new test centre is expected to open in 2025.  These two new test centres will include facilities for full emissions testing and will meet all of the current and known future requirements and their design will be used as a template for any additional new test centres, subject to further consideration and funding. The European Commission recently recommended a diesel emissions Particle Number (PN) test for younger vehicles, as an alternative to the current diesel smoke test. This is because younger vehicles, which due to their design may produce high levels of hazardous particulate matter if not properly maintained, are unlikely to fail the diesel smoke test. Following initial trials of PN testing equipment, the DVA is developing detailed proposals for the reintroduction of a fully compliant diesel emissions test for cars and light vehicles and is undertaking preparatory work for investment in two further new test centres.  These proposals include a combination of PN testing for younger vehicles first registered from 1 January 2013 at existing test centres and diesel smoke testing for older vehicles at newly constructed test centres. The introduction of PN testing would require a statutory consultation process and an amendment to domestic legislation.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, pursuant to AQO 2673/17-22, AQO 1877/17-22, AQO 749/17-22, AQW 1385/17-22; and in light of the Assembly motion approved on 17 May 2021; and the Fair Employment (School Teachers) Bill NIAR 19-2022; what progress has been made in removing the exception of teachers from the Fair Employment and Treatment (Northern Ireland) Order 1998.
answer_text: The Fair Employment (School Teachers) Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 will come into operation on 12th May 2024. It will ensure that teachers have the same protection from discrimination as other workers.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister what action has been taken to omit (i) Article 71 of the Fair Employment and Treatment (Northern Ireland) Order 1998; and (ii) Regulation 30 of the Fair Employment and Treatment Order (Amendment) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2003.
answer_text: The Fair Employment (School Teachers) Act (NI) 2022 provides for the revocation of Article 71 of the Fair Employment and Treatment (Northern Ireland) Order 1998 and Regulation 30 of the Fair Employment and Treatment Order (Amendment) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2003.  The Act comes into operation automatically on 12th May 2024.  The effect of revocation of these provisions is to ensure that teachers have the same protection under the relevant legislation as other workers.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his Department has a wild bird response plan for Avian Flu.
answer_text: DAERA does not have a separate formal response plan for HPAI in wild birds. However, many of the key constituent parts of a HPAI response plan for wild birds are in place: targeted carcass collection and testing, consistent messaging and advice, and scoping of surveillance and population data evidence needs to inform conservation actions.   NIEA has produced guidance for conservation site managers in NI dealing with HPAI outbreaks in wild bird populations.	My officials continue to monitor developments with HPAI in our wild bird population.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he will seek to extend the role of the Animal Welfare Committee to matters outside of slaughter.
answer_text: DAERA is informally consulted on the work of the Animal Welfare Committee (AWC) and receives full access to the advice and support that the Committee provides to Defra and the other devolved administrations. Outside of slaughter matters we can consider any recommendations from the AWC whilst also ensuring any proposals which we progress can be adapted in a way that best suit our needs. I have no plans to change this arrangement but will continue to work hand in hand with the AWC to ensure that their expert knowledge and advice is utilised in advancing animal welfare standards in Northern Ireland.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what consideration has been given to installing (i) permanent speed indication devices; (ii) speed limit reminder signs; and (iii) other traffic calming measures, on the B90 road at Greenisland village.
answer_text: Speed indication devices are provided by Policing and Community Safety Partnerships (PCSPs) rather than by my Department so requests for them should be addressed to the local PCSP in the first instance.My Department does not install speed limit repeater signage within 30 mph zones where there is a street lighting system present. I am advised that traffic calming measures are already installed on the B90 Station Road, and the B90 Upper Road would not be considered suitable for traffic calming measures due to the large volume of traffic using it.Should you have any further questions on this matter, please contact DfI Roads Northern Division at: dfiroads.northern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what consideration has been given to reviewing the speed limit on the B90 at Greenisland village.
answer_text: At present, my Department has no plans to review the existing 30 mph speed limit on the B90 at Greenisland village, nor have we received any recent requests to do so.  Should you have any further questions on this matter, please contact DfI Roads Northern Division at: dfiroads.northern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) what progress has been made in reviewing the eligibility criteria for Free School Meals and Uniform Grants; and (ii) when public consultation on these issues will commence.
answer_text: The Department is currently undertaking a review of Free School Meals and Uniform Grant eligibility criteria.  The review has considered a wide range of options that include the provision of a universal free school meals to some/all year groups as well as raising the income thresholds that apply to the eligibility criteria for applicants in receipt of Universal Credit or Working/Child Tax Credit. It is also actively considering the option for guidance on the costs of school uniforms to be on a statutory basis. My Department continues to work to develop a costed options paper and it is intended that a public consultation exercise will take place later in the year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice what is the target time for scheduling a public inquiry at the Planning Appeals Commission following referral by the Department for Infrastructure.
answer_text: The Planning Appeals Commission (PAC) is an independent tribunal body with case management and progression a function of the Chief Commissioner. It would therefore not be appropriate for me as Minister of Justice to comment on matters that encroach on that independence.You may wish to contact the Chief Commissioner directly on this matter

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice what priority and extra resource is given to the Planning Appeals Commission when dealing with Regionally Significant planning applications.
answer_text: The Planning Appeals Commission (PAC) is an independent tribunal body with case management and progression a function of the Chief Commissioner. It would therefore not be appropriate for me as Minister of Justice to comment on matters that encroach on that independence.I can however advise that Northern Ireland Courts and Tribunals Service (NICTS) acts as the sponsor for PAC, providing administration support to the Commission under the terms of an agreed Memorandum of Understanding (MoU). Commission resources are included in the MOU with PAC to consist of whatever number of Commissioners is deemed appropriate by the Department in consultation with the Chief Commissioner. A recent recruitment exercise increased the Commission numbers.You may wish to contact the Chief Commissioner directly on this matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what work has been carried out to identify the schools that are most lacking natural ventilation in their classrooms, in order to provide those classrooms with purifiers with HEPA filtration units to combat COVID-19.
answer_text: All schools have received guidance in relation to ventilation, and supplied with CO2 monitors to assist in identifying areas where ventilation needs to be improved. Schools with concerns about ventilation have been advised to contact the EA maintenance helpline for advice/support on the best approach for their individual circumstances. An EA Maintenance Officer will attend the school within 24hrs (the next day) following a call to the maintenance helpline. During the visit the Maintenance Officer will identify remedial work required (anything from advising to open windows through to replacement windows, or filtration). Any work arising from the visit will be treated as ‘Priority Immediate Response’ and is afforded the highest priority. In some cases where an area of poor ventilation has been identified, it may be appropriate to consider the use of an air cleaning unit as an additional mitigation whilst further remedial work is undertaken to improve ventilation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education will she ask the Public Health Agency for further direction on the efficacy of placing HEPA air filtration units in school classrooms in light of the results of the David Hume Foundation study in the Marche region of Italy, published in March 2022.
answer_text: Current guidance from the Public Health Agency, the Department of Health and SAGE is that the most effective mitigating measure in relation to Covid-19 is natural ventilation. My Department and the Education Authority are supporting schools in prioritising the effective implementation of this measure.All schools have received guidance in relation to ventilation, and supplied with CO2 monitors to assist in identifying areas where ventilation needs to be improved.  Schools with concerns about ventilation have been advised to contact the EA maintenance helpline for advice/support on the best approach for their individual circumstances. My Department has noted the David Hume Foundation study and will maintain a review of this area. The Department continues to follow the guidance on ventilation and air conditioning in the workplace provided by the Public Health Agency.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how many HEPA air filtration units have been supplied to schools during the COVID-19 pandemic.
answer_text: 200 HEPA air filtration units have been supplied to schools during the COVID-19 pandemic. The Education Authority Maintenance Service received requests from 59 schools for air filtration units. All 59 schools were assessed on site by maintenance staff, resulting in 46 schools requiring air filtration units.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to AQO 2787/17-22, whether she will undertake a public consultation exercise as part of the pre-legislative policy development process on reforming blasphemy laws.
answer_text: As I have indicated previously, I am of the view that the common law offences of blasphemy and blasphemy libel are archaic and that they have no place in a modern society. I am committed to freedom of and from religion and remain sympathetic to removing such outdated and unused offences from the law.   An exercise is currently underway to identify legislative priorities across my Department and officials are in the process of developing options for a draft legislative programme for the new mandate for my consideration.While that process is ongoing, and in the absence of an Executive and a sitting Assembly, I am not in a position to say when a policy consultation on the removal of blasphemy offences might be taken forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline the DfI Roads policy that ensures road signs, particularly in rural areas, are visible and not covered by overgrown vegetation.
answer_text: The primary emphasis of my Department’s policy relating to tree and hedge maintenance is on road safety rather than for aesthetic or amenity purposes. At times, in the interests of the safety of road users, my Department carries out maintenance work on trees, hedges and verges in order to remove obstructions to sight lines and traffic signs that may endanger road users. My Department seeks to work with landowners to proactively manage safety issues in this way, including by placing notices in the local press and in farming journals reminding owners/occupiers of their obligations to maintain their lands. My Department carries out regular routine maintenance inspections on all roads during which inspectors will note trees and hedges, within or immediately adjacent to the road boundaries, which are causing an obstruction, obscuring road signs, or have the potential to create danger for the road user. The priority given to undertaking remedial action will take account of the risk to road safety and this will include responding to ad-hoc enquiries from road users on all matters of road safety, including the visibility of road signs which may be fully or partially obscured, faded or experiencing a loss of reflectivity.

answer_text: My Department does not maintain the triangular metal Irish mileage markers along the A2 Coast Road but does endeavour to ensure that the remaining markers are not damaged during routine maintenance activities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the cost of teacher substitutions in the (i) 2019/20; (ii) 2020/21; and (iii) 2021/22 financial years.
answer_text: The total cost of teacher substitutions for grant-aided schools (excluding voluntary grammar) in Northern Ireland in the financial year (i) 2019/20 was £72,178,147.38, in (ii) 2020/21 was £83,577,323.69 and in (iii) 2021/22 was £100,656,112.50. The figures above are management information and were obtained from the Teachers’ Pay and Pensions System.
